cowos.summary: CoWoS map is comprehensive and well-sourced at 31 nodes. Primary issues are a stale ticker (Shinko 6967.T delisted June 2025), an internal contradiction on Micron HBM4 TCB supplier (BESI 'exclusive' vs hbm.json's Hanmi-primary/BESI-second), and a missing note on HBM4 base-die-on-logic upstream change. Single-point flags are correct and well-documented.

hbm.summary: HBM map is the most current and well-researched of the five topics, with strong HBM4 / base-die-on-logic coverage. Three actionable issues: TSV node grade is monopoly when it should be oligopoly (three captive producers); Lam Research TSV tooling exclusivity lacks a ⚑ flag; hybrid bonding 2026 validation by SK Hynix makes the '2027 start' framing stale. Overall high quality.

substrate.summary: Substrate map is well-structured and current, covering all three tiers (ABF/BT/glass) in 14 nodes. Main gaps: Ajinomoto ABF film node lacks ⚑ flag (false negative vs. other maps); Shinko ticker is stale (delisted 2025/6); Absolics/SKC glass substrate entrant is absent. Node count at 14 is below the 19-node band floor.

plp.summary: PLP map is well-constructed at 26 nodes with strong CoPoS timing detail. Main issues: RDL node slightly under-grades TSMC's current dominance; Rubin/NVIDIA CoPoS link is speculative and should be qualified; ABF market share framing for total-vs-high-performance creates a misleading competitive picture. Map is appropriately tagged as 'emerging' for glass and RDL stages.

material.summary: Materials map covers 17 nodes across front-end and packaging material segments with generally good currency. Critical issue: Cabot Microelectronics / Entegris are listed as if two separate companies under the same ENTG ticker — they are the same entity post-2022 acquisition. Warning issues: JSR's JIC nationalization supply-risk is understated; SK Siltron/Doosan acquisition status needs verification as of June 2026; 中砂 N2 80% share claim is self-flagged as unconfirmed but still tagged as supply_chain_lock:tight.

advanced.summary: Advanced (2nm GAA) topic is well-constructed with 26 nodes and accurate ⚑ flags on ASML EUV, KLA metrology, and 家登 EUV Pod. Two grade issues: eq-metro and eq-reticle both graded 'monopoly' when the market structure is duopoly/oligopoly. No false-negative ⚑ on true monopoly nodes. EUV mask blank duopoly (AGC/Hoya) could use a ⚑ flag on m-mask.

fe-equipment.summary: fe-equipment has one critical error: the d-foundry demand node is mis-graded 'monopoly' when foundry is structurally oligopoly (TSMC + Samsung + Intel). Additionally, t-pod (EUV Pod) is graded monopoly when it is a documented duopoly (家登+Entegris). The u-mask-blank AGC/Hoya ~93% duopoly is missing a ⚑ flag. Other issues are minor accuracy and link issues.

metrology.summary: Metrology topic is generally well-executed with rigorous ⚑ gates. Key issues: t-ate is graded 'monopoly' when ATE is a duopoly (Advantest + Teradyne ~85% combined); t-slt analysis is inconsistent with the more rigorous downgrade in ate.json. Lasertec's two-node presence (mk-inspect + mk-blank) is legitimate and correctly structured.

ate.summary: ATE topic is the most rigorously audited topic in the cluster — the critic-reviewed downgrade of致茂 SLT from ⚑ to competitive oligopoly is methodologically sound. The single ⚑ (LEENO probe pins) has a 'med-high' rather than 'high' confidence level which is a minor consistency issue. Overall high quality with 18 nodes (at the lower target band but complete).

mature-node.summary: Mature-node topic is well-structured with 22 nodes. Soitec SOI/RF-SOI is the single ⚑ and correctly graded monopoly. Main issue: f-cn (China mature foundry) is graded 'highgrowth' when 'redocean' better describes the multi-player price-war structure. Geopolitics/trade-policy timeliness should be updated to reflect any Section 301/232 actions taken by mid-2026.

memory.summary: The 24-node memory map is well-sourced and current through Q1 2026. Two critical issues: (1) WDC ticker on all NAND nodes is outdated — the flash business is now SanDisk/SNDK post Feb 2025 spin-off; (2) dual-#2 labeling in m-enterprise-ssd is an internal contradiction that needs correction. The missing WFE layer for NAND manufacturing is a structural gap versus sibling topics.

lpddr.summary: The 17-node LPDDR map is logically sound and well-timestamped through H1 2026. The identical golden picks to hbm.json are independently defensible but warrant a clarifying note to prevent template-copy misreading. Minor structural gap: Winbond's automotive LPDDR path is not wired to the n-auto demand node.

ufs.summary: The 15-node UFS map has a correct market structure assessment (no structural lock-throughs) but suffers from zero v1.3 company grading, making it unable to surface top picks despite declaring v1.3 framework. Critical fixes: (1) wrong ticker on u-iot Samsung/Kioxia combo entry, (2) WDC→SNDK for NAND node, (3) merchant controller grading needs to be populated.

cxl.summary: The 13-node CXL map has strong sourcing and current timing (Intel DR delay, AMD Venice 2026 correctly modeled). Two critical issues: (1) n-rcd competition=monopoly is factually wrong for a 3-player ~40/40/10 split market — should be oligopoly; (2) Apacer ticker 2469.TW is wrong (FLEXium), should be 8271.TW. The near-absence of v1.3 grading (only 2 of ~25 company entries graded) means ALAB and MRVL are missing from top-pick evaluation despite being natural candidates.

asic.summary: ASIC map is the most complete and best-sourced in the compute cluster (23 nodes, v1.3 framework, detailed ⚑ for ARM Neoverse / Broadcom / TSMC N3-N2). Two competition-grade over-calls (foundry and CoWoS nodes both labeled monopoly instead of oligopoly) and a missing ABF film ⚑ are the main gaps. Broadcom's deliberate exclusion from 💎 pool due to VMware diversification is valid but undocumented.

icdesign.summary: icdesign map is well-structured with correct dual ⚑ flags (ARM CPU IP monopoly + NVIDIA AI GPU monopoly) and good 2026 currency (Synopsys-Ansys close confirmed, RISC-V 25% penetration cited). Main gap is the foundry node over-graded 'monopoly'; node count of 15 is below target band but intentional. No missing key players identified.

fpga.summary: FPGA map has two critical gaps: (1) zero v1.3 company grading across all 22 nodes — no 💎 picks can be derived by the engine, making the map investment-opaque; (2) GUC ticker is wrong (5269.TW should be 3443.TW) in two nodes. Three foundry/packaging nodes are over-graded 'monopoly'. Altera ownership note should clarify USD denomination. The ⚑ setup for Microchip RTG4 (rad-hard Flash FPGA) is well-researched and accurate — just needs grading fields added to unlock 💎 derivation.

mobile-ap.summary: Mobile-AP map is the best-structured and most internally consistent in the cluster (16 nodes, v1.3 framework, correct foundry grade as oligopoly). ARM CPU/GPU IP ⚑ is well-supported. Main gaps: Samsung Exynos 2600 deployment conflict between d-captive and f-foundry source, Samsung LSI mid-range segment missing, and ARM IP price-hike claim should be caveated as reported. No critical structural issues.

auto-soc.summary: Auto-SoC map correctly identifies ARM CPU IP as the sole single-point (vehicle dual-source requirements eliminate chipmaker monopolies). Key structural gap: foundry layer entirely absent — the 2021-2022 auto chip shortage was a foundry-capacity crisis, making this a meaningful omission for risk analysis. STM ticker bundled under TXN is a factual error. Mobileye's 'Intel 子公司' label is outdated post-2022 IPO. MCU destocking cycle (2024-2025) context missing from growth narrative.

dc-networking.summary: dc-networking is the most comprehensive map in the cluster (19 nodes, 70+ companies) with strong 2025-2026 currency. Main issues are 3 grade over-calls (u-foundry, retimer-pcie, dsp-optical all tagged 'monopoly' when oligopoly fits the schema definition better) and a minor demand-node grade anomaly. ⚑ flags are well-justified and sourced. The SMTC monopoly-vs-emerging apparent contradiction across topics is contextually valid.

transceiver.summary: Transceiver map is well-structured for a 15-node chain with strong 2025-2026 sourcing, especially for the EML laser lockup narrative. The InP substrate ⚑ decision is defensible but borderline given AXT's China production exposure. Key gap: no Arista in demand node; 1.6T DSP single-source risk needs time-dimension disclosure.

cpo.summary: CPO map has strong 2025-2026 sourcing and good structural coverage of 19 nodes. Critical gap: Himax MLA sole-supplier status for TSMC COUPE Gen1/2 is explicitly stated in analysis but lacks ⚑ flag — clear false negative. FOCI FAU also needs ⚑ for COUPE segment. NVIDIA Quantum-X CPO ship status should be confirmed. NVIDIA-Marvell $2B deal attribution to CPO engine node overstates the confirmed scope.

siph.summary: siph is the most detailed map in the cluster (19 nodes, 80+ companies, deep Taiwan ecosystem coverage). Main audit flags: Himax MLA ⚑ false negative (same as CPO); TSMC COUPE foundry node grade should be oligopoly not monopoly at the full node level; Sivers Semi incorrectly tagged core_business:true despite being pre-scale with yield issues. f-modul/f-module naming collision risk. Overall currency is excellent (2026 TrendForce, IMEC, GF sources).

copper.summary: Copper map is well-scoped for AI rack interconnect copper/connector chain. The SMTC monopoly(dc-networking) vs emerging(copper) apparent discrepancy is correctly justified by different product-market contexts — not a real contradiction. Credo AEC ⚑ is the map's single well-supported lock. Node count 16 is below design target but appropriate for the genuinely shorter copper chain. No critical issues.

power.summary: power.json is well-maintained and 800V architecture transition is correctly framed with good timeliness (Delta ±400V 2026 shipping, 800V 2027 ramp). Main gaps: Vicor absent from VRM node, TE Connectivity country label ambiguous, and the Blackwell-era PSU share framing needs refresh to Rubin-era. CDU ⚑ flags are correctly absent given 5+ competitor structure. Missing 💎 candidates: MPWR (VRM ~70% Rubin share, core_business:true, supply_chain_lock:tight) and Delta 2308 (power rack, cold plate dual-track, core_business:true, supply_chain_lock:tight) are both structurally qualified for 💎 derivation.

datacenter.summary: datacenter.json is structurally strong with the b-turbine ⚑ flag well-justified and unique within the DC cluster. Two material accuracy issues: Vertiv Q1/Q4 2026 earnings label ambiguity, and the GlobalEnergyMonitor source for gas turbine cartel is the wrong citation (Brazil steel article). Google's $175-185B capex figure appears significantly overstated vs public guidance. Missing 💎 candidates: 華城 1519.TW (LPT node: tight lock, core_business:true) and Vertiv VRT (multiple nodes with tight lock, core_business:true across several nodes).

Missing 💎: ASPEED 5274.TWO (monopoly grade, core_business:true, tight lock — strongest 💎 candidate in the cluster) and Foxconn 2317.TW (Houston node, tight lock).

Missing 💎: EMC 2383.TW (tight lock, core_business:true, monopoly grade ⚑) and Mitsui Mining 5706.T (tight lock, elephant role ⚑) are the strongest 💎 candidates but both are flagged as elephants/multi-business — EMC qualifies as a pure-play 💎.

passive.summary: passive.json has two competition grade misapplications: cap-tantalum incorrectly graded 'monopoly' (should be oligopoly) and d-ai-server demand node incorrectly graded 'monopoly' (should be oligopoly/highgrowth). The BaTiO₃ ⚑ source is weak (single corporate website). The 'Vitec' unconfirmed Yageo subsidiary should be cleaned up. nuclear.summary: Nuclear topic is well-researched with strong 2025-2026 source currency. Critical flaw: stats node count (25) does not match actual node count (17). HTS tape near-⚑ status (Faraday Factory) is a borderline false negative worth addressing.

Amorphous monopoly grade is defensible but global oligopoly is more accurate.

The Crusoe ⚑ exclusivity framing needs updating post-Oracle/OpenAI expansion cancellation. GPU grade should be oligopoly not monopoly. Node count (15) is below target band — networking/storage layers are missing. Nebius ARR guidance ($7-9B) is aspirational vs. $1.6B Q1 run-rate.

The process is captive to TSMC in this map context; oligopoly is defensible only if counting Samsung/SK Hynix internal processes, but in the CoWoS chain TSV is essentially TSMC-exclusive.

issues.basis: f-tsv companies list shows SK Hynix + Samsung + Micron all doing in-house TSV — definitionally oligopoly not monopoly. Monopoly = one firm.
issues.fix: Change competition grade from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly'; add single flag if warranted for Lam Research TSV tooling dependency

issues.finding: Lam Research is described as 'HBM TSV 獨家' (exclusive) for Samsung/SK Hynix — this is a strong claim that merits a ⚑ single flag, but the node eq-tsv does not have a 'single' field. Lam's SABRE 3D/Syndion near-exclusivity for HBM TSV equipment is a genuine single-point risk.
issues.basis: Node analysis: 'Lam Research 在 TSV 蝕刻與電鍍領域近乎獨佔'; source cited: https://inf.news/en/tech/93e514891b03af13b5df9e4ea8305fb8.html

issues.finding: Hybrid bonding node description says 'HBM4E（2027）起逐步導入 hybrid bonding'. However, hbm.json's own f-stack node (sourced 2026-04) states 'SK Hynix 完成 12-Hi hybrid bonding HBM 驗證（2026/4）' and the eq-hb node itself mentions SK Hynix HBM4 early supply. The timing is no longer 2027+ — hybrid bonding in HBM is validating in 2026 for HBM4, with HBM4E (not HBM4) as the first ramp node being debated. The framing '2027起' understates the 2026 acceleration.
issues.basis: f-stack node: 'SK Hynix 完成 12-Hi hybrid bonding HBM 驗證（2026/4）'; Hanwha source: https://www.digitimes.com/news/a20250917PD242/hanwha-hybrid-bonding-sk-hynix-hbm4-2027.html
issues.fix: Update eq-hb desc/analysis: 'SK Hynix validated 12-Hi hybrid bonding for HBM4 in 2026/4; HBM4 16-Hi may still use MR-MUF for yield reasons; HBM4E 2027+ = first mass ramp for hybrid bonding'

issues.finding: The logic base die node correctly documents TSMC N5/N3P for HBM4/4E. However it lists GUC (3443.TW) with supply_chain_lock:'tight' and core_business:true for 'C-HBM4E 設計服務'. GUC's role is design services for TSMC's custom HBM offering — it is a design house, not a manufacturing single point. Flagging it with tight lock overstates the investment thesis.
issues.basis: GUC is a design services subsidiary of TSMC; the C-HBM4E is a TSMC platform product — GUC facilitates but does not own the manufacturing chokepoint
issues.fix: Change GUC supply_chain_lock from 'tight' to 'med'; update note to clarify design services role vs. manufacturing

issues.finding: Node f-rdl (RDL redistribution layer) is graded 'oligopoly'. Given that TSMC CoPoS is still in pilot line (2026/6 completion) and the only other players at RDL scale are ASE (FOPLP 600mm) and Samsung (Watch SoC only), this market is currently a practical duopoly at most, with TSMC dominant. 'Oligopoly' understates TSMC's current dominance — 'highgrowth' or maintaining oligopoly with a note would be more accurate.
issues.basis: TSMC CoPoS pilot line 2026/6 completion per TrendForce; ASE FOPLP 600mm in equipment stage; Samsung FOPLP only at Watch SoC scale
issues.fix: Keep oligopoly but add ⚑ flag or dominance note: 'TSMC CoPoS 2026 試線主導，ASE/Samsung 為第二梯隊 2027+'

issues.finding: The demand node describes 'NVIDIA Rubin 復角尺 5.5×' as driving PLP adoption. 'Rubin' in the context of 2026 refers to the next-gen GPU after Blackwell. However Rubin's packaging has not been confirmed for CoPoS/FOPLP — it is a candidate, not a confirmed customer. Framing it as a CoPoS driver without qualification overstates certainty.
issues.basis: As of 2026-06, Rubin GPU packaging specs are not publicly confirmed for panel-level. TSMC CoPoS targets 2028-2029 mass production — Rubin generation timing overlaps but is not confirmed.
issues.fix: Change note to 'Rubin Ultra (CoPoS candidate, >2028, packaging not yet confirmed)' rather than implying it is a committed customer

The 40%/25% figure appears to be for all ABF market segments, not the high-performance IC substrate ABF that matters for PLP. This could mislead readers into thinking there is meaningful competition.

issues.fix: Clarify in analysis: '整體 ABF 市場 AFT ~40%，但高效能 IC 基板 ABF（先進封裝必要規格）市佔 >95%；Sekisui 二供仍在驗證'

issues.finding: Node p-cmp lists 'Cabot Microelectronics (CMC Materials)' under ticker ENTG. This is incorrect: Entegris acquired CMC Materials in July 2022 for $6.5B, and the CMC ticker CCMP was delisted. But Entegris and CMC/Cabot are different business lines — the CMP slurry leadership historically associated with 'Cabot Microelectronics' is now part of Entegris's product portfolio. Listing both 'Cabot Microelectronics (CMC Materials)' and 'Entegris' under the same ticker ENTG creates a duplicate/confusion: the node implies two separate companies but they are now one.
issues.basis: Entegris 2022/7 acquisition of CMC Materials for $6.5B — CCMP delisted; note in node itself: '[2022/7 Entegris 收購 CMC Materials $6.5B, CCMP delisted]'
issues.fix: Rename company to 'Entegris (incl. CMC Materials CMP slurry)'; remove duplicate entry; single ENTG ticker

issues.finding: JSR Corporation is listed as '#1 ~22%' with note '2024/6/25 自 TSE 下市 (JIC ¥900B 收購)'. This is accurate for the delisting. However JSR is now a fully private entity under JIC (Japan Investment Corporation) — listing it without a ticker or with '—' is correct (node does show '—') but the company section should note that JSR's strategic direction under JIC may prioritize Japanese national semiconductor interests, which is a supply-security risk factor for non-Japanese customers (Intel, TSMC, Samsung).
issues.basis: JSR privatization under JIC ¥900B; JIC is a government-backed fund — this introduces strategic/geopolitical supply risk
issues.fix: Add to JSR note: '國有化後受 JIC 戰略管控，供應安全風險（特別是非日客戶）'

issues.finding: EUV mask blank node (m-mask) lists Hoya as 'mask blank 領先' but AGC+Hoya duopoly is covered in fe-equipment (u-mask-blank) with share data (~59%/~34%). issues.finding: Node eq-metro is graded 'monopoly' with KLA at 55-60% overall inspection/metrology share. This overstates concentration: the node bundles optical inspection, overlay, CD-SEM, and X-ray metrology — a sum-of-sub-markets where KLA does NOT reach monopoly in every segment (CD-SEM: Hitachi High-Tech ~60%; overlay: KLA + ASML YieldStar dual-player; X-ray: Bruker + Rigaku). KLA's 73.8% figure is overall metrology+inspection revenue share but individual tool categories are oligopolistic, not monopolistic.
issues.basis: metrology.json explicitly grades m-overlay as oligopoly (KLA+ASML dual), m-cdsem as oligopoly (Hitachi ~60%), m-xray as oligopoly. KLA overall market share does not equal monopoly in every sub-category.
issues.fix: Re-grade eq-metro from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly'. KLA has a near-monopoly specifically in patterned wafer bright-field inspection (80%) which is correctly flagged as ⚑ in metrology.json's i-bf node, but the combined metrology node should be oligopoly.

issues.finding: Node eq-reticle (EUV Pod) is graded 'monopoly' but the node itself acknowledges '家登 + Entegris 為共同 ASML 認證的雙占'. A duopoly (two ASML-certified suppliers) is oligopoly, not monopoly. The 家登 >80% pod share makes it a near-monopoly within the EUV pod sub-segment but Entegris is a genuine #2 certified supplier.
issues.basis: Node analysis states: 'EUV Pod 主要供應僅家登 + Entegris 兩家（雙占）'. Also fe-equipment t-pod correctly notes 'Entegris 為共同認證 #2，雙占格局'.
issues.fix: Consider changing competition grade to 'oligopoly' to accurately reflect the duopoly structure. The ⚑ flag is appropriate for 家登's dominant >80% position but the market structure grade should be oligopoly (2 certified suppliers).

issues.fix: No fix needed. Confirm this is correctly graded.

issues.finding: BSPD node describes Intel Panther Lake mass production in 'Q4 2025' but Intel faced delays — Panther Lake volume ramp was pushed to 2026. The description says '2025 Q4 Panther Lake' which may need verification.

issues.fix: Verify Intel Panther Lake actual mass production timing; update if volume production was 2026 rather than 2025 Q4.

issues.finding: CRITICAL: d-foundry (晶圓代工先進邏輯) demand node is graded 'monopoly'. This is factually wrong — the foundry demand side has TSMC ~70% + Samsung Foundry + Intel Foundry as explicit listed companies. TSMC is the leading logic foundry but foundry is structurally oligopoly (TSMC/Samsung/Intel/GF). Grading the CUSTOMER/DEMAND node as monopoly is also conceptually incorrect — competition grades should describe the supply-side market structure of the node.
issues.basis: Schema: 'competition describes the NODE's market structure, not the individual company'. The foundry node lists TSMC (先進邏輯 >70%), Samsung Foundry, and Intel Foundry — three companies, structural oligopoly. TSMC at 70%+ share in advanced logic is high concentration but NOT monopoly; Samsung and Intel both have active GAA programs.
issues.fix: Change d-foundry competition from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly'. TSMC's >70% advanced logic share is dominant oligopoly, not monopoly (Samsung SF2P and Intel 18A are commercially active alternatives).

issues.finding: t-pod (EUV/High-NA POD) is graded 'monopoly'. As with advanced.json's eq-reticle, this is a documented duopoly (家登 >80% + Entegris as co-certified ASML supplier). The ⚑ flag correctly represents 家登's dominance but 'monopoly' overstates the market structure.
issues.basis: Node analysis: '家登 3680 為全球 EUV pod >80% 領導...（Entegris 為共同認證 #2，雙占格局）'. Two ASML-certified suppliers = duopoly within oligopoly.
issues.fix: Change competition from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly'. The ⚑ flag on 家登's >80% dominant position remains valid.

However, this raises the question of whether the mk-blank ⚑ is also visible in the stats header. Stats shows '3' single points with subtitle listing 'EUV 光罩檢測 + blank' as one combined bullet. Verify stats count correctly reflects both mk-inspect AND mk-blank as separate ⚑ nodes.

issues.finding: t-ate (ATE) node is graded 'monopoly' with Advantest AI chip ATE >85%. However, Teradyne is explicitly listed as a second ATE supplier with ~30% overall share and stated capability for AI chips. Per the brief, ATE is Advantest+Teradyne ~85% combined. The market structure is duopoly (oligopoly), not monopoly — Advantest's >85% in AI GPU test specifically is a customer-configuration lock, not a global capability monopoly (Teradyne can test other AI chips). The ate.json topic correctly grades a-soc as 'oligopoly' and notes 'Teradyne 有能力'.
issues.basis: metrology.json t-ate single: 'Advantest AI 晶片測試市佔 >85%（NVIDIA / AMD 商用 GPU）...整體 ATE Advantest ~58% (2024) + Teradyne ~30% 寡占 ~80%'. ate.json explicitly grades SoC ATE as oligopoly and uses ⚑ only for LEENO probe pins. ate.json note: 'Teradyne 有能力測其他 AI 晶片 → 客戶配置而非能力獨佔'.
issues.fix: Re-grade t-ate from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly'. The ⚑ flag can remain for Advantest's NVIDIA-specific customer lock but market structure is duopoly (Teradyne is a real #2 with AI chip testing capability). This is consistent with ate.json's more nuanced treatment.

issues.finding: t-slt in metrology.json grades competition as 'oligopoly' and flags 致茂 as 'NVIDIA GPU SLT 獨家', citing 'Kill test: NO — NVIDIA Rubin/Blackwell ramp 不能換廠'. However, the ATE topic (ate.json) explicitly downgraded this to a competitive oligopoly via critic review: 'Teradyne Titan HP (2025/10) + Cohu 具備 SLT 能力, 致茂的鎖定屬客戶集中 + 商業誘因不足而非全球能力獨佔'. The metrology.json t-slt analysis has not been updated to reflect this critic ruling, creating an inconsistency across the two topics.
issues.basis: ate.json a-slt node: '致茂降為競爭性寡占（客戶集中風險，非 ⚑ 能力鎖喉）'. metrology.json t-slt still has full ⚑-gate language ('過 Gate: A3 ✓...Kill test: NO') without the critic caveat.
issues.fix: Update metrology.json t-slt analysis to add the critic caveat: Teradyne Titan HP (2025/10) and Cohu have SLT capability, so 致茂's lock is customer-configuration (not global capability monopoly). Keep oligopoly grade but remove the implied ⚑-gate passage or add 'critic: downgraded to competitive oligopoly per ate.json critic review'.

issues.finding: f-cn (China Mature Foundry) is graded 'highgrowth'. This is an unusual grade for a foundry node — 'highgrowth' describes the growth trajectory but not the market structure (concentration/competition). The China mature foundry segment has SMIC, Hua Hong, Nexchip, Yangtze Memory (YMTC), and many others — this is a 'redocean' or 'oligopoly' market structure (many players, state-subsidized price war). 'highgrowth' is not one of the valid competition grade categories as described in the brief (monopoly/oligopoly/highgrowth/redocean/emerging).
issues.basis: Brief schema: competition = one of: monopoly / oligopoly / highgrowth / redocean / emerging. 'highgrowth' IS a valid enum but it describes market dynamics, not structure. For a foundry with 5+ players it seems misapplied.
issues.fix: Consider changing f-cn competition from 'highgrowth' to 'redocean' (many players, state-subsidized price war, commoditized) or 'oligopoly' if the intent is to describe SMIC/HuaHong/Nexchip as the top 3. The subtitle itself says '殺價搶單' which signals redocean.

issues.fix: No fix on grade. Verify the GF 6-K SEC source URL is accessible.

issues.finding: 3D NAND wafer node has empty upstream[]. The memory.json design has no equipment/WFE row, so the 3D NAND manufacturing process (high-aspect-ratio etch, ALD/CVD deposition — Lam, AMAT, TEL) is invisible. This is a deliberate scope choice but leaves the most capital-intensive upstream stage un-mapped. By contrast, lpddr.json and ufs.json both include WFE rail nodes.
issues.basis: Structure comparison with sibling topics lpddr.json (n-wfe node) and ufs.json (u-etch, u-depo nodes). NAND manufacturing requires identical equipment categories.
issues.fix: Add a WFE rail row with Lam/AMAT/TEL nodes and wire n-vnand upstream to it, OR explicitly note in the subtitle that WFE is out of scope for this map to set user expectations.

issues.finding: Phison ticker is 8299.TW throughout memory.json. Phison Electronics (群聯電子) is listed on Taiwan's OTC market (TPEX/GreTai 上櫃), so the correct Bloomberg/standard ticker suffix is 8299.TWO. The ufs.json sibling file already uses 8299.TWO correctly.
issues.basis: Comparison between memory.json (8299.TW) and ufs.json (8299.TWO); Phison is an 上櫃 stock, not TWSE main board.
issues.fix: Replace all 8299.TW with 8299.TWO in memory.json to match ufs.json and actual market listing.

issues.finding: NAND market shares cite Q4'25 data (Samsung 28%, SK Hynix 22.1%). These figures are current and appropriate for June 2026. BiCS10 332L for Kioxia FY2026 mass production is correctly noted. No stale framing detected.
issues.basis: TrendForce Q4'25 NAND revenue report cited in sources.
issues.fix: No action needed.

issues.finding: u-base-die (HBM4 base die foundry) competition=monopoly. This is defensible: TSMC is the sole external foundry for HBM4 base die (SK Hynix 100% + Micron HBM4E 2027); Samsung Foundry is in-house only. The monopoly grade applies to the merchant/external foundry market for this specific logic die, not the broader foundry industry. The detailed kill-test rationale in the analysis field is appropriately thorough.
issues.basis: Node analysis and single-flag text in the file. Consistent with v1.3 framework where 'near-monopoly' single-source dependency can be graded monopoly.
issues.fix: No change needed; the kill-test rationale in analysis adequately qualifies the grade.

issues.finding: LPDDR golden picks (ASML + 000660.KS + MU) are identical tickers to hbm.json golden picks. The brief flags this as a possible template-copy. Upon analysis, the LPDDR single-point logic is INDEPENDENTLY VALID: (1) ASML EUV is genuinely required for 1γ DRAM process used in LPDDR5X/6 — this is correct and distinct from HBM's EUV dependency (LPDDR is die-level, HBM uses same but different context); (2) SK Hynix 000660.KS and Micron MU are correctly the two pure-play DRAM companies in the cartel with core_business:true, while Samsung is correctly excluded (too diversified, node_role='elephant'). However, the coincidence of identical tickers warrants a reviewer note: ASML's role in LPDDR is upstream equipment, not a direct LPDDR supplier — an investor may mistakenly read ASML as an LPDDR-specific pick rather than a cross-cutting equipment pick.
issues.basis: Cross-file comparison of lpddr.json vs hbm.json golden pick derivation. Both assign ASML to EUV litho node with identical logic. Logic is valid but the UX implication (ASML appears as 'LPDDR pick') is potentially misleading without context.
issues.fix: Add a note in n-litho node or subtitle clarifying that ASML appears as a single-point in multiple DRAM maps because EUV is a cross-cutting process gate, not LPDDR-specific. This prevents the template-copy perception while the logic is retained.

issues.finding: Winbond (auto LPDDR4/4X) and CXMT both feed only into n-pop. Winbond's car-grade LPDDR4/4X should logically flow to n-auto demand (车用 ADAS). The current edge map routes Winbond → n-pop → n-phone only, missing the automotive demand node.
issues.basis: n-winbond analysis states 'car-grade LPDDR4/4X'; n-auto demand node exists but no edge connects Winbond to it. n-auto only has upstream from n-dram.
issues.fix: Add edges: n-winbond → n-auto and update n-auto upstream to include n-winbond.

issues.finding: SOCAMM2 node describes SK Hynix 192GB as '2026/4 量產' (mass production). The TrendForce source cited is dated April 2026, so this is current. NVIDIA Vera Rubin is described correctly as the key demand driver. However, the node does not mention the SOCAMM2 vs CAMM2 standardization path or JEDEC status, which is a mild omission for a 2026 map.
issues.basis: Node analysis; public JEDEC standardization timeline for SOCAMM2 was still in progress as of early 2026.
issues.fix: Minor: add a note on JEDEC standardization status of SOCAMM2 to flag the spec-lock risk.

issues.finding: n-dram competition=oligopoly despite having a single flag (structural cartel). This is correct per the v1.3 framework: the node has a ⚑ flag for the cartel dynamic, while the competition grade describes the market structure (3-player oligopoly, not monopoly). Samsung is excluded from golden picks via node_role='elephant' (correct: multi-segment conglomerate, not a pure DRAM play). SK Hynix and Micron's inclusion as core_business:true is appropriate.
issues.basis: Schema logic: competition grade = node market structure; ⚑ flag = single-source dependency. Both can coexist.
issues.fix: No change needed.

issues.finding: Node u-iot (IoT/Wearable/Industrial eMMC) has a combined entry 'Samsung / Kioxia（eMMC）' with ticker 005930.KS (Samsung). This incorrectly applies Samsung's ticker to a dual-company entry that includes Kioxia (285A.T). The entry should either be split into two separate company records with correct tickers, or the ticker field should reflect only the primary company.
issues.basis: 005930.KS is Samsung's Korean Stock Exchange ticker. Kioxia's correct ticker is 285A.T (Tokyo Stock Exchange). Using 005930.KS for a 'Samsung/Kioxia' combined entry is factually incorrect for Kioxia.
issues.fix: Split the combined entry into two: (1) Samsung eMMC 5.1 ticker=005930.KS; (2) Kioxia eMMC ticker=285A.T.

Using WDC ticker for a NAND supply chain node is factually incorrect post-split.
issues.basis: Same corporate event as memory.json finding. SanDisk Corporation (SNDK) is the correct entity for NAND operations.
issues.fix: Update to 'SanDisk Corporation' with ticker SNDK in u-nand-us.

issues.finding: ZERO v1.3 company grading fields (core_business, supply_chain_lock) exist in ufs.json. issues.basis: Python scan of all ufs.json company objects confirms zero core_business or supply_chain_lock fields. Compare to lpddr.json (3 graded) and cxl.json (2 graded) which at least have partial grading.
issues.fix: Grade key merchant companies: Silicon Motion (SIMO) at u-merchant (core_business=true, supply_chain_lock='tight' candidate given ~55-65% of merchant UFS TAM), and Phison (8299.TWO) at u-merchant. Note: since no UFS node has a ⚑ flag, no company will qualify as 💎 regardless — but the grading should still be applied for completeness and future re-evaluation if node single-flags change.

issues.finding: u-auto has upstream only from u-captive. Merchant controller path (Silicon Motion Ferri-UFS, Phison automotive eMMC with ASPICE CL3) is not connected by edge to u-auto. The u-auto analysis text correctly mentions merchant automotive controllers, but the data model does not reflect this.

issues.finding: Phison ticker is 8299.TWO in ufs.json (correct) but 8299.TW in memory.json (inconsistent). The UFS file is correct; the memory file should be updated to match.

issues.finding: JEDEC UFS 5.0 standard published February 2026 is correctly noted. First devices projected Q4 2026 is consistent with public JEDEC announcement. This is current and accurate.

issues.finding: Node correctly notes 'Apple iPhone 用自研 NVMe-like 控制器、不採 JEDEC UFS' — Apple is excluded from the UFS demand chain. This is accurate: Apple uses its own proprietary NVMe-based storage protocol on iPhone/iPad, not JEDEC UFS. This is a positive accuracy point.
issues.basis: Industry-known Apple storage architecture. No UFS chips in iPhone/iPad.
issues.fix: No change needed.

issues.finding: n-rcd competition=monopoly. The node itself describes a 3-player market: Montage ~40-45%, Rambus ~40-45%, Renesas ~10-15%. Per the audit schema, monopoly=5 maps to a single supplier. A 3-player JEDEC-cartel market with ~40/40/10 share split is oligopoly (=4), not monopoly. The ⚑ single flag correctly captures the structural lock (no customer can escape the cartel), but the competition grade should be oligopoly to accurately reflect the multi-player nature. The DB node (n-db) correctly uses oligopoly for the same 3-player structure.
issues.basis: Schema definition: monopoly=1 player. Node analysis explicitly states Montage ~40-45%, Rambus mid-40%, Renesas ~10-15%. Sibling node n-db uses oligopoly for the same three suppliers — internal inconsistency.
issues.fix: Change n-rcd competition from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly'. The ⚑ single-flag already conveys the cartel lock; the grade change corrects the factual mislabeling without weakening the investment signal.

issues.finding: Apacer 宇瞻 is listed with ticker 2469.TW. The correct TWSE/TPEX ticker for Apacer Technology Inc. (宇瞻科技) is 8271.TW (confirmed by memory.json and ufs.json both using 8271.TW with the note 'TWSE listed since 1997'). TWSE code 2469 belongs to FLEXium Interconnect (光鋐科技), a completely different company (flexible PCB manufacturer).
issues.basis: Cross-file comparison: memory.json m-tw-module and ufs.json u-module both use 8271.TW for Apacer with consistent notes. 2469.TW is FLEXium per TWSE company listing.
issues.fix: Change Apacer ticker in cxl.json n-modtw from 2469.TW to 8271.TW.

issues.node: multiple (n-rcd, n-db, n-mrcd, n-ckd, n-spd, n-pmic-s, n-cxlmem, n-cxlsw, n-idm, n-modtw, n-cpu, n-demand)
issues.finding: Only 2 companies in cxl.json have v1.3 grading fields (Montage 688008.SS and Rambus RMBS, both in n-rcd). All other nodes have zero core_business/supply_chain_lock grading. ALAB in particular is the CXL market leader with the only named hyperscaler deployment (Azure) and is a strong candidate for core_business grading.

issues.finding: Intel Diamond Rapids delay to 2027 is correctly noted as the main MRDIMM headwind. AMD Venice 2026 as the near-term MRDIMM platform is also correctly identified. The analysis is current and reflects the known platform timeline as of mid-2026. One omission: Renesas's claim of 'industry's first complete Gen2 MRDIMM chipset' is noted without flagging that Montage contests this with their own Gen2 ES samples sent to Samsung/SK Hynix.
issues.basis: Node analysis cites Renesas RRG50120 as 'industry's first complete Gen2' per Renesas press release; Montage counter-claim is in Montage node but the competitive framing could be tighter.
issues.fix: Minor: add cross-reference in n-mrcd analysis that both Renesas and Montage claim Gen2 MRDIMM leadership; customers will validate in 2026.

issues.finding: Astera Labs Leo is described as '2026 H2 初量產' with note 'main business is still Aries retimer'. This is accurate as of mid-2026. However, the node does not mention that CXL memory expansion TAM is highly dependent on CPU platform ramp (AMD Venice 2026 / Intel Diamond Rapids 2027) — the demand-side timing risk for Leo is primarily platform-dependent, not technology-limited. This context exists in n-cpu but not n-cxlmem.
issues.basis: Intel Diamond Rapids delay noted in n-cpu affects Astera Leo's addressable market for H2 2026.
issues.fix: Add a note in n-cxlmem: 'Leo TAM 2026 primarily gated by AMD Venice ramp; Intel Diamond Rapids delay limits 2026 upside to Venice-only deployments'.

issues.finding: n-db (Data Buffer) is not flagged ⚑, while n-rcd (same 3 suppliers, same JEDEC lock) is. The analysis explains this is intentional: the cartel lock is represented once on n-rcd to avoid duplicate flagging. This is a deliberate design choice consistent with the v1.3 framework guidance.
issues.basis: n-db analysis: '結構與 RCD 同屬三雄 cartel，但本節點列 oligopoly（cartel 鎖喉以 RCD 節點代表標示，避免重複打旗）'. Correct methodology.
issues.fix: No change needed.

issues.finding: Node competition graded 'monopoly'. TSMC holds ~80% of AI ASIC wafer starts, but Samsung SF2P and Intel 18A are both structurally capable alternatives — TSMC's dominance is demand-side concentration, not a supply-capability monopoly. The correct grade is 'oligopoly' (per audit checklist: foundry nodes graded monopoly are a common error).
issues.basis: Industry-standard definition: monopoly = single viable supplier; TSMC's AI ASIC share is ~80% but Samsung and Intel 18A are confirmed alternatives (Samsung: Tesla AI6 ₩22T order; Intel 18A: MSFT/AWS partial). Cross-check with checklist rule.
issues.fix: Change competition from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly' on node fp-foundry.

issues.finding: Node competition graded 'monopoly' for CoWoS / Advanced Pkg. TSMC CoWoS-L is dominant but Amkor EMIB and ASE FOCoS are listed as alternatives within the same node — by definition this is oligopoly, not monopoly. A monopoly grade should reflect zero viable alternatives.
issues.basis: Node itself lists TSMC + Amkor + ASE as co-suppliers. Monopoly is inconsistent with multi-company node listing.
issues.fix: Change competition from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly' on node fp-pkg.

issues.finding: Missing ⚑ false-negative: Ajinomoto ABF film is cited as '95-98% 獨佔' within the fp-substrate analysis text yet the node itself carries no ⚑ single-point flag. The ABF film node is a textbook single-point (ASML EUV-tier equivalent in substrate materials). The ⚑ should be on the ABF film supplier (Ajinomoto), not the substrate fabricators.
issues.basis: Node analysis explicitly states '味之素 ABF 膜 95-98% 獨佔'; ABF film is a widely cited single-point in substrate supply chains.
issues.fix: Either add a dedicated ABF film node with ⚑, or add a ⚑ flag to fp-substrate citing Ajinomoto ABF film as the sub-tier single point (with confidence=high, 2-source citation).

issues.finding: Broadcom node lacks core_business / supply_chain_lock v1.3 grading fields (only has node_role:'elephant'). By the schema, a 💎 top-pick derivation requires core_business:true AND supply_chain_lock:'tight' AND a real ticker AND ⚑. Broadcom is the most prominent ASIC single-point — absence of grading fields means it cannot surface as a 💎 in the engine, which is likely intentional (see note about VMware diversification) but should be documented.
issues.basis: Schema spec: 💎 = node has single AND company has core_business:true AND supply_chain_lock:tight AND real ticker. ds-broadcom.companies[0] has node_role:'elephant' but no core_business or supply_chain_lock.
issues.fix: If AVGO is intentionally excluded from 💎 pool due to VMware diversification, add an explicit comment field. Otherwise add core_business:true and supply_chain_lock:'tight' to enable engine derivation.

issues.finding: Tesla Dojo shutdown framing ('2025/8 砍 Dojo') is plausible but the node bundles Tesla with OpenAI/Anthropic in a single demand node. Tesla AI5/AI6 (Samsung SF2) has different supply-chain implications than the OpenAI/Anthropic ASIC story — mixing them dilutes the ⚑ analysis at each relevant upstream node (Broadcom for OpenAI, Samsung for Tesla).
issues.basis: Structural observation from node design.
issues.fix: Minor: consider splitting Tesla into a separate demand node or adding a cross-reference to the Samsung foundry path for AI5/AI6 distinct from the Broadcom ASIC path.

issues.finding: Node count 15 is below the 19-31 design target band. The map intentionally cross-references HBM/CoWoS/substrate topics rather than duplicating them. The core IC-design chain (EDA → IP → Fabless → Foundry → Demand) is complete and all nodes are reachable. Flag for awareness but not a structural break.
issues.basis: Audit checklist: flag <19 as info unless chain is genuinely complete. Chain is complete with cross-references noted.
issues.fix: Consider adding 1-2 packaging-layer stub nodes (CoWoS, InFO) as rail nodes with explicit cross-topic links to maintain visual completeness, if the engine supports inter-topic linking.

issues.finding: Node competition graded 'monopoly'. Same over-grade issue as asic/fp-foundry: TSMC advanced logic is ~70% share but Samsung Foundry GAA and Intel Foundry 18A are listed in the adjacent m-others node as viable alternatives. The node analysis itself correctly states this is the 'most important lock-throat' but the grade should be 'oligopoly' per the audit rule that foundry nodes must not be graded monopoly.
issues.basis: Audit checklist rule: foundry nodes graded monopoly are a common error. Samsung/Intel alternatives are acknowledged in m-others.
issues.fix: Change competition from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly' on node m-tsmc.

Its placement in a residual 'other fabless' bucket understates its relevance and may confuse cross-topic navigation.

issues.fix: Add a cross-reference note to Lattice entry pointing to fpga topic, or move it to a separate networking/FPGA rail node.

issues.finding: ARM Neoverse / Cortex correctly flagged ⚑ with confidence:high and multi-source citation. No false-negative here. The single flag in stats (v:2) correctly counts ARM CPU IP and AI GPU as the two single-points. Both are properly graded 'monopoly'. This is the strongest ⚑ setup in the cluster.
issues.basis: Positive confirmation — no action needed.
issues.fix: No fix required.

issues.finding: Note says 'Tesla AI5 量產規劃見 d-other 註' and references GUC as potential design-service partner, but the d-other node explicitly clarifies 'Tesla 自研 in-house design'. The cross-reference is consistent but the phrase in f-asic-design analysis could be read as implying GUC is a Tesla design partner, which is unconfirmed and disclaimed in d-other.

issues.fix: Remove or clarify the 'Tesla AI5 量產規劃見 d-other 註' in f-asic-design analysis to avoid implying GUC / TW design-service involvement.

issues.finding: ZERO v1.3 grading on ALL company entries across all 22 nodes. No company in fpga.json has core_business, supply_chain_lock, or node_role fields. This means the 💎 top-pick engine cannot derive any picks from this map. The map is structurally sound but completely ungraded at the v1.3 company level.

issues.fix: Grade every company in the map following v1.3 schema. Priority candidates for core_business:true + supply_chain_lock:tight: (1) Microchip RTG4 at v-microchip (has ⚑, should be 💎); (2) AMD at v-amd (FPGA market leader ~40-50%, node has no grading); (3) Lattice at v-lattice (low-power FPGA dominant). Microchip RTG4 should be the map's only 💎 given its ⚑ + real ticker MCHP + tight lock.

issues.finding: GUC (創意電子) is listed with ticker '5269.TW' in the p-svc node. The correct TWSE ticker for Global Unichip Corp (GUC / 創意電子) is 3443.TW, confirmed by TWSE, Yahoo Finance, CNYES, and all other maps in this repo (asic.json and icdesign.json both use 3443.TW correctly).

issues.finding: Altera company entry has ticker '—' (unlisted). Altera was spun out of Intel and Silver Lake acquired 51% in April 2025 (deal closed September 12, 2025 per Intel 8-K). Altera is now an independent private company (Silver Lake 51% / Intel 49%), not a publicly traded entity. The ticker '—' is correct for now, but the note should reflect the current ownership and the final close date accurately.
issues.basis: Intel 8-K filed 2025-09-12 (SEC EDGAR); CNBC 2025-04-14: Intel to sell 51% to Silver Lake at $4.46B / $8.75B valuation. fpga.json v-altera note says 'Silver Lake 控股；$44.6 億 51%' — amount is correct but note uses 億 (hundred millions) ambiguously; '$44.6 億' could be read as TWD — clarify as USD $4.46B.
issues.fix: Update v-altera note to: 'Silver Lake 51% / Intel 49%，2025/09/12 完成交割，企業估值 ~$87.5 億美元 (USD)；Agilex 9/7/5/3 系列；Azure MANA SmartNIC 主供'.

issues.finding: GUC listed with ticker '5269.TW' in ip-tw node as well (same wrong-ticker issue appears in two nodes: ip-tw and p-svc). Both must be corrected to 3443.TW.

issues.fix: Change GUC ticker in ip-tw.companies from '5269.TW' to '3443.TW'.

issues.finding: Node competition graded 'monopoly'. Consistent cluster-wide error: TSMC high-end FPGA foundry share is high, but Samsung Foundry (Lattice Avant 16nm FinFET) and Intel Foundry (Altera Agilex) are active alternatives for different product lines. Even for AMD/Xilinx high-end products, the node analysis itself says 'Samsung/Intel 技術上可承接，切換需 24-36 月重新驗證' — this is the definition of oligopoly with switching costs, not monopoly.

issues.fix: Change competition from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly' on node f-tsmc.

issues.finding: Advanced packaging node (CoWoS/EMIB) graded 'monopoly'. Node lists TSMC CoWoS, ASE (委外), and Intel EMIB as suppliers. Multiple suppliers = oligopoly by definition. CoWoS capacity constraint is a demand-side bottleneck, not a monopoly.
issues.basis: Node companies[]: TSMC + ASE + Intel Foundry. Three suppliers cannot be a monopoly.
issues.fix: Change competition from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly' on node p-adv.

issues.finding: Microchip RTG4 correctly flagged ⚑ with 'near-monopoly · QML Class V Flash rad-hard FPGA for deep space / human-rated missions' — best-supported ⚑ in this map. issues.finding: Versal Premium Gen2 (TSMC N6) is referenced with '2026Q1 sample' — this matches known timing. issues.finding: Google Tensor G5 described as '2025 起全客製設計，轉單台積電 N3 + InFO'. This is accurate per ChromeUnboxed citation. However, Samsung LSI (005930.KS) is listed with Exynos 2600 on 'SF2 2nm' — as of 2026-06-10, Exynos 2600 Galaxy deployment status is uncertain (Samsung confirmed SF2 2nm for Exynos 2600 but some markets still use Snapdragon for Galaxy S26 Ultra). The PhoneArena source cited says 'Galaxy S26 Ultra chip to be produced by TSMC only' which would mean the SF2 Exynos 2600 is not in Galaxy S26 Ultra at all, contradicting the node framing.

More critically, Samsung LSI is missing from the d-entry / mid-range segment entirely — Samsung's Exynos 1480/1580 are significant Android mid-range SoCs competing with MediaTek Dimensity 8-series and Qualcomm Snapdragon 7-series. issues.fix: Add 'reported' qualifier: '傳 ARM 評估核心 IP 漲價最高 +300%（Android Headlines 2025/01 報導，ARM 未官方確認）'.

issues.finding: Foundry / manufacturing layer is entirely absent from auto-soc map. All SoC/MCU nodes connect directly from design to demand with no foundry intermediate node. For the compute cluster, auto SoC foundry involves: TSMC (ADAS SoC, advanced), GlobalFoundries / TI internal (MCU, specialized), Samsung (MCU), TSMC mature node (Renesas, NXP). The auto supply chain disruption of 2021-2022 was precisely a foundry-layer crisis — omitting it makes the map strategically incomplete for risk analysis.
issues.basis: Structural gap: edges[] shows direct connections from design nodes (a-highend, a-mid, mc-mcu) straight to demand (d-tier1, d-oem) with no foundry intermediate. Other maps in the cluster all include foundry rows.
issues.fix: Add a foundry/manufacturing row with at least 2 nodes: (1) Advanced foundry for ADAS SoC (TSMC N5/N7/N3, Samsung SF3) and (2) Mature foundry for MCU (GlobalFoundries 180/130nm, ON Semi, TI fab, TSMC mature). Connect from design nodes to foundry nodes to demand nodes.

issues.finding: STMicroelectronics (STM) is listed only in aggregate 'TI / Microchip / STM' within mc-mcu with ticker TXN. STM is a distinct publicly-traded company (ticker: STM on NYSE/Euronext) and is a top-3 automotive MCU supplier by volume (STM8 / STM32 widely used in auto ECUs). Grouping STM under TXN's ticker is a ticker accuracy error.
issues.basis: STM (STMicroelectronics) NYSE ticker: STM. Market position: one of the top automotive semiconductor suppliers globally, separate from TI (TXN). Industry consensus places STM in top-5 auto MCU alongside NXP/Infineon/Renesas/TI.
issues.fix: Separate STM into its own company entry with ticker 'STM' in mc-mcu.companies, and add Microchip Technology (MCHP) separately. Remove the bundled 'TI / Microchip / STM' entry or split it into three distinct entries.

issues.finding: Microchip Technology (MCHP) is listed only in the bundle 'TI / Microchip / STM' under mc-mcu with no individual ticker. Microchip is a significant auto MCU supplier (PIC32 / SAM series, plus acquired Atmel auto MCUs and Microsemi). It also has a dedicated ⚑ in the FPGA map (Microchip RTG4 rad-hard). Should have its own entry with ticker MCHP.
issues.basis: Microchip Technology NASDAQ ticker: MCHP. Auto MCU market participant via PIC/AVR/ARM Cortex-M devices.
issues.fix: Add separate Microchip Technology entry with ticker MCHP to mc-mcu.companies.

issues.finding: Mobileye is described as 'EyeQ 曾近 70% 但被侵蝕'. This is accurate directionally. However, Mobileye's ticker is MBLY (NASDAQ) — correctly shown in node. The 'Intel 子公司' label is outdated: Mobileye went public as an independent company in October 2022 (IPO NASDAQ: MBLY). issues.basis: Mobileye MBLY IPO October 2022, NASDAQ. Intel ~88% majority shareholder but Mobileye is an independent public company with its own governance.
issues.fix: Update Mobileye note from 'Intel 子公司' to 'NASDAQ MBLY 獨立上市（Intel ~88% 股權）'.

issues.finding: Black Sesame 黑芝麻 ticker listed as '2533.HK' (HKEX). This is correct. However, the note says 'passes US Commerce/Defense reviews for global sales' (美商務部審查取得全球銷售許可). This is a meaningful 2026 development that should be more prominently flagged — it changes the competitive landscape for non-Chinese markets. The Digitimes source is cited correctly.

issues.fix: Elevate the US approval fact to the main desc or analysis rather than just the note field, as it has significant investment implications for MBLY/AMBA competitive positioning.

issues.finding: Node does not reflect the 2025 automotive semiconductor inventory correction and subsequent MCU pricing pressure. NXP, Infineon, Renesas, STM all experienced significant revenue declines in 2024-2025 auto MCU as the post-COVID inventory correction unwound. This context affects the growth narrative for the control layer.

issues.fix: Add timeliness note to mc-mcu: '2024-2025 汽車 MCU 進入去庫存週期（NXP/Infineon/Renesas/STM 均下修），2026 下半年 SDV + 線控 zonal 需求回升為潛在催化劑'.

issues.finding: ARM Cortex-R/A ⚑ is well-argued and correctly identifies Quintauris RT-Europa (RISC-V JV by NXP/Infineon/Bosch/Nordic/Qualcomm) as the only structural challenge, correctly dated to 2028+ for production ASIL-D readiness. This is the strongest auto-sector IP analysis in the cluster. No false-negative here.

issues.fix: No fix required — ⚑ setup for ARM vehicle CPU IP is accurate and current.

issues.finding: Node competition graded 'monopoly' but the node lists TSMC, Samsung Foundry, and Intel Foundry as companies. Even for leading-edge AI ASIC/DSP, the market is structurally oligopoly (TSMC >90% but not 100%; Samsung GAA 3nm already supplies Google TPU; Intel 18A targeting 2026/2027 ramp). The ⚑ single text correctly says 'TSMC is leading-edge single point' but the competition grade should be 'oligopoly' per the schema rule (monopoly = 1 firm). The ⚑ flag is valid as a capacity/lead-time single-point, but grade mismatch with schema definition.
issues.basis: Schema definition: 'monopoly=5 means one firm'; node itself lists 3 foundries with Samsung already shipping Google TPU 3nm. Audit checklist: 'foundry nodes graded monopoly is common error'.
issues.fix: Change competition from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly'. The ⚑ single-point flag can be retained as TSMC COUPE/leading-edge AI specific chokepoint, but grade description must match node-level market structure.

issues.finding: Node graded 'monopoly' but the analysis explicitly states 6 firms hold 92% combined share: Astera Labs, Parade, Credo, Renesas, Montage, Microchip/TI. A 6-firm market is oligopoly, not monopoly. No single firm has >50% share.
issues.basis: Analysis text: 'Astera Labs 為 PCIe 5/CXL hyperscaler 主供…前 6 家合計 92%'. Multiple named competitors. Schema rule: monopoly = one firm.
issues.fix: Change competition from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly'. Remove the monopoly grade; no ⚑ flag exists here so no cascade impact.

issues.finding: Node graded 'monopoly' but analysis says Marvell has >60% 800G DSP share with Broadcom, MaxLinear, Semtech, and Credo as named competitors. A 5-firm market where the leader has 60% (not 100%) is oligopoly, not monopoly.
issues.basis: Analysis: 'Marvell (前 Inphi) 為 800G PAM4 DSP >60%…MaxLinear + Semtech + Credo LRO/LPO 補位'. Marvell share stated as '>60%', not exclusive.
issues.fix: Change competition from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly'. No ⚑ flag on this node so no cascade impact.

issues.finding: ASPEED ~70-80% BMC share with Nuvoton and Lattice as named alternatives. 70-80% leader share in a 3-firm market with real alternatives is oligopoly with dominant leader, not textbook monopoly. Grade 'monopoly' is a slight over-grade at the node level (node-level market structure, not per-company).
issues.basis: Analysis: 'ASPEED AST2700 ~70-80%...新唐 4919 次要'. 20-30% held by alternatives means the NODE is not a monopoly market.
issues.fix: Consider changing to 'oligopoly' with a dominant player note, or retain 'monopoly' only if the intent is to signal near-monopoly economics (document the decision). The ⚑ flag on ASPEED is well-supported.

issues.finding: Alphawave Semi listed with ticker QCOM and described as 'Qualcomm 子公司 (2025/12/18 收購完成)'. The Qualcomm acquisition of Alphawave was announced but faced UK CMA scrutiny. As of early 2026 the acquisition timeline was extended. The note says 'ahead of schedule' but this needs verification — if the deal closed, AWE.L ticker should indeed be delisted.
issues.basis: Qualcomm announced $2.4B acquisition of Alphawave Semi in 2024; CMA Phase 1 review completed Dec 2024 with remedies. Claimed completion 2025/12/18 per note.
issues.fix: Verify deal closed status. If confirmed closed Dec 2025, update ticker from QCOM to 'QCOM (ex-AWE.L)' or note 'acquired by QCOM' clearly. If still pending, revert to AWE.L.

issues.finding: Demand node graded 'monopoly'. Demand nodes represent end-customers not a supply-side market, so competition grading is conceptually inapplicable. The analysis describes 6 hyperscalers with >75% concentration — if anything this is 'oligopoly' not 'monopoly' even for demand-side analysis.
issues.basis: Schema states competition describes node's market structure. Demand nodes have multiple customers — 5+ named hyperscalers.
issues.fix: Change demand node competition to 'highgrowth' or 'oligopoly' to reflect multi-buyer concentrated demand, consistent with how cpo/siph handle their demand nodes.

issues.finding: SMTC (Semtech) graded as 'Active Copper / LR Linear' with share 'FiberEdge linear redriver'. In dc-networking topic, Semtech is at a node graded 'monopoly' (dsp-optical). Per the brief's specific question: in copper topic, SMTC is at emerging=1 (ACC-MSA co-chair for linear EQ). This is NOT a contradiction — Semtech is in a different role in each topic: in dc-networking it's a minor DSP/LPO participant at a node dominated by Marvell; in copper it's in the ACC linear EQ segment which is genuinely new/emerging. The node-level grade difference is justified by the different market contexts.
issues.basis: dc-networking node 'dsp-optical' is about PAM4 DSP where Marvell leads >60%. copper topic 'cbl-acc' is about ACC linear EQ, a new 2026 standard. SMTC's role differs: redriver/LPO supplementary in dc-networking vs. ACC-MSA co-chair for a new emerging spec in copper. No contradiction.
issues.fix: No fix required. The grade difference is contextually correct. However, dc-networking should clarify Semtech's role as 'LPO/LRO redriver' not full DSP, since Semtech does not make a full PAM4 DSP chip — it makes linear drivers/redrivers. Consider moving Semtech to a separate LPO/linear redriver node if granularity increases.

issues.finding: InP substrate node is NOT flagged ⚑ even though AXT holds 60-70% of supply with its production in China under export license control (Q4 2025 shipment delays confirmed). The analysis explains the reasoning (Sumitomo/Coherent as Western alternatives = not absolute single point) but the China-geopolitical chokepoint on AXT's 60-70% share constitutes a 'critical single-point' risk under a supply disruption scenario. The ⚑ decision is defensible but borderline — should at minimum be documented as a monitored risk.
issues.basis: Analysis correctly notes AXT 60-70% but China-gated; AXT Q4'25 export delay source cited. The audit checklist calls InP a chokepoint analog to Shin-Etsu ABF.
issues.fix: Either add ⚑ flag with caveat 'geopolitical single-point (China export license risk on AXT 60-70%); Western alt sources Sumitomo/Coherent provide 12-18m substitution', or add a dedicated risk note to the analysis text. At minimum flag this as a monitored ⚑ candidate.

issues.finding: DSP node is not ⚑ flagged (analysis correctly justifies: Marvell >60% but Broadcom serves as verified second source for some hyperscalers). However, Marvell's Ara 1.6T DSP is a near-lock on 1.6T module timelines in 2026 — no alternative has been qualified at 1.6T speed as of data. For 1.6T specifically this is closer to a time-limited single point than the analysis implies. Not a false negative at the 800G level, but worth noting timeliness gap for 1.6T qualification.
issues.basis: Analysis: 'Broadcom 為部分 hyperscaler 已驗證的第二源' — this appears to be for 800G, not 1.6T Ara platform.
issues.fix: Add a time-dimension note: 'At 1.6T Marvell Ara is de-facto single source through 2026; Broadcom 1.6T DSP ETA uncertain. Monitor 1.6T qualification status quarterly.'

The node note correctly hedges this but the fact that this is in the f-engine (CPO optical engine) node could mislead: Marvell's $2B is primarily about silicon/ASIC NVLink integration, not confirmed CPO engine supply.
issues.basis: NVIDIA-Marvell $2B deal (April 2026): primary stated purpose is NVLink Fusion custom XPU silicon; CPO noted as 'potential long-term extension' per reports.
issues.fix: Reposition Marvell in f-engine with a clear note: 'NVIDIA $2B investment primarily for NVLink Fusion XPU silicon; CPO role speculative/long-term extension not confirmed as of 2026-06-10.'

issues.finding: Himax MLA for TSMC COUPE Gen1/Gen2 is described as 'COUPE Gen1/2 唯一' (sole supplier) and analysis says it's a '鎖喉點'. Yet the node has NO ⚑ single-point flag. issues.finding: FOCI 上詮 is described as 'COUPE Gen1/2 唯一' FAU supplier, and Browave is listed with supply_chain_lock:'tight' despite only being 'NVIDIA認證中' (certification in progress). The node itself has competition:'highgrowth' which is correct but FOCI's sole-supplier status for COUPE Gen1/Gen2 should earn the node an ⚑ flag for the TSMC COUPE segment, similar to how CPO map handles it.

issues.finding: TSMC COUPE correctly flagged ⚑ as 'PIC Foundry / SiPh Platform' sole CPO platform. issues.fix: Add ⚑ single='客戶獨家 · Himax NIL MLA 為 TSMC COUPE Gen1/2 唯一供應（信心度 high · Hunterbrook + Ming-Chi Kuo 雙源）'. Add core_business:true, supply_chain_lock:'tight' to Himax company. Consistent fix with CPO map.

issues.finding: Sivers Semi listed with core_business:true, supply_chain_lock:'tight' but the analysis describes Sivers as a '被忽略 InP chokepoint' with 'sub-30% yield' and only WIN Semi as HVM partner. issues.fix: Remove core_business:true and supply_chain_lock:'tight' from Sivers Semi entry. Add note 'nascent chokepoint; yield risk precludes 💎 grade at current maturity'. This prevents erroneous 💎 derivation.

issues.finding: The node analysis states 'IQE (UK, GaAs-epi 整體龍頭, 2025 ~18%)' but IQE's actual GaAs epi share is likely higher (IQE historically claims ~35-40% of compound semiconductor epi wafer market). The ~18% figure may refer to a narrower segment. IQE has had financial difficulties (profit warnings 2022-2024) and the share figure should be verified.

issues.fix: Clarify the market definition for IQE's 18% share (GaAs photonics sub-segment vs. all compound semiconductor epi). Verify against IQE's own market share disclosures.

issues.finding: Demand node mentions 'NVIDIA Quantum-X InfiniBand 2026H1' and 'Spectrum-X Ethernet 2H26'. As of 2026-06-10, Quantum-X IB was targeted for 2026H1 — this timeline should now be checkable. Consistent issue with CPO map.

issues.finding: SMTC (Semtech) grade discrepancy analysis (per the brief's specific question): In dc-networking topic, Semtech appears at the 'dsp-optical' node graded 'monopoly' (over-graded, as noted above). The grade difference correctly reflects market maturity differences. NO fix needed on the copper side.

issues.fix: No fix needed for copper. Fix dc-networking's dsp-optical grade from monopoly to oligopoly (separate finding above). The SMTC discrepancy is a dc-networking grade error, not a copper mapping error.
