However CPU LGA sockets also ship to enterprise servers, cloud servers outside the NVLink rack context. The chain is incomplete for the full socket market but appropriate for this AI copper-centric map.
issues.basis: Map scope is AI datacenter copper; enterprise server socket market is out of scope by design.
issues.fix: No structural fix needed; add a scope note to socket node: 'scope limited to AI accelerator rack context; enterprise server LGA market excluded from this map'.

issues.finding: Delta's PSU share is listed as '70% Blackwell' but the current 2026 platform is Vera Rubin / GB300. The Blackwell framing should be updated to explicitly state 'Blackwell-era majority, Rubin ramp in progress'. ±400V is currently shipping and 800V is queued for 2027 — the node name 'Server PSU (12V/48V/HVDC)' should prominently mention ±400V as the live shipping SKU for 2026, not just HVDC as a future concept.
issues.basis: Delta TechNews source in the JSON itself: '±400V 今年出貨、800V 蓄勢 2027 年放量'
issues.fix: Update f-psu analysis and marketSize to distinguish: ±400V as current 2026 shipping product; 800V as 2027 ramp. Rename node or add 'PoE48V→±400V→800V roadmap' to desc.

issues.finding: The '800V Rack' framing is correct directionally. However Rubin Ultra / Kyber is referenced as '2027 量產' in the desc but '2026H2 起跑' in marketSize. Kyber is NVIDIA's codename for the rack system; confirmation of Rubin Ultra Kyber 2027 vs 2026H2 should be clarified. The contradiction is within the same node.
issues.basis: Node desc says '2026H2 起跑'; Rubin Ultra Kyber ~2027 per multiple sources including the NVIDIA blog cited.
issues.fix: Clarify: Vera Rubin NVL72 H2 2026 = start of Rubin family; Rubin Ultra Kyber 600kW-1MW = 2027 target. Separate the two milestones.

issues.finding: CDU ⚑ flag audit: The t-coldplate node does NOT carry a ⚑ flag and this is CORRECT — 4 competing suppliers (AVC, Cooler Master, Jentech, Delta/Auras dispute) means no single supplier meets the ≥75% threshold. The node correctly notes the 4-supplier lock by NVIDIA. Auras (3324) is listed as 4th supplier candidate but also appears in t-vc as the '3D VC 龍頭'. The text correctly notes the Digitimes vs GTC dispute on 4th supplier. No false negative.
issues.basis: Schema: ⚑ requires near-monopoly/customer-exclusive with single supplier. 4-supplier split prevents flagging.
issues.fix: No change needed. Confirm the Auras-vs-Delta dispute note remains current; add a refresh date.

issues.finding: CDU node (c-cdu) has no ⚑ flag, which is correct given 5+ competitors. However, Vertiv's 11.3% share claim as '#1 liquid cooling' should note this is overall liquid cooling market, not specifically CDU. The Eaton/Boyd note says 'announce 2025/11/3, close 2026/3/12' — this is confirmed and current. Ecolab/CoolIT close in March 2026 is also correctly noted. No CDU-specific false negative ⚑.
issues.basis: MarketsandMarkets cited for 11.3% figure; 5+ capable CDU suppliers confirmed.
issues.fix: Clarify Vertiv 11.3% is 'overall liquid cooling market share', not CDU-only. Minor label fix.

issues.finding: TE Connectivity ticker listed as 'TEL' (country: CH). The correct NYSE ticker is 'TEL' — this is actually correct (TE Connectivity traded as TEL on NYSE). However listing country as 'CH' (Switzerland/Schaffhausen domicile) may confuse users who expect 'US'. The note acknowledges Schaffhausen HQ. Minor labeling issue but could cause ticker lookup errors since TEL is not a Swiss exchange symbol.
issues.basis: TE Connectivity trades as TEL on NYSE; incorporated in Ireland but operational HQ in Schaffhausen.
issues.fix: Consider changing country to 'IE' (domicile) or 'US' (primary listing) with a note. The ticker TEL is correct for NYSE.

issues.finding: Vicor is listed in the audit brief as a key VRM/power-stage player but is absent from the power.json. Vicor's Factorized Power Architecture (FPA) is referenced by NVIDIA for high-density power delivery. While not a full ⚑, Vicor is a notable omission from ic-vrm and f-psu nodes.
issues.basis: Audit brief domain focus lists 'VRM/power-stage (MPS MPWR/Infineon/Renesas/Vicor)'. Vicor not found in any node.
issues.fix: Add Vicor (VICR, US) to ic-vrm companies as 'Power component 補位' with note on FPA architecture for AI server high-density power delivery.

issues.finding: The f-grid node is graded 'oligopoly' which is correct. However GoodWe (688390.SS) is listed as 'global top 3 hybrid inverter' citing Wood Mackenzie 2020 data — this is stale (6 years old). In 2025-2026, GoodWe faces intensified competition from Sungrow, Huawei, and SolarEdge. The 2020 ranking may no longer be accurate.
issues.basis: Source cited: 'Wood Mackenzie 2020' — outdated for 2026 audit context.
issues.fix: Either refresh the GoodWe market position with 2025 data or flag the 2020 source as potentially stale. GoodWe's relevance to AI DC is marginal anyway (the note says 'potential supplier' only).

issues.finding: The stats header shows '獨佔/客戶獨家單點: 0' with empty sub. Given the audit domain focus specifically asks to verify CDU ⚑ flags — no CDU node carries ⚑, which is structurally correct. However, the Jentech CoWoS lid near-monopoly (~100% per t-heatsink description) should potentially be re-examined. The t-heatsink node text says '健策 CoWoS lid ~100%' with core_business:true and supply_chain_lock:tight but no ⚑ flag. In the broader context the CoWoS lid is covered in the CoWoS supply-chain topic — the power.json intentionally scopes only thermal components — so no ⚑ is the correct call for this topic scope.
issues.basis: Schema note: ⚑ requires the WHOLE node supply to be locked, not just one company within it. Jentech coexists with AVC, Cooler Master, Delta on cold plate.
issues.fix: No change to stats needed. Correct that ⚑ count = 0 given topic scope limitations.

issues.finding: The LPT node lists Hitachi Energy with ticker '6501.T' (which is Hitachi Ltd. parent). Hitachi Energy is not independently listed — it is a subsidiary of Hitachi Ltd. (6501.T). The ticker reference could mislead investors into thinking Hitachi Energy is a pure-play. Hitachi Energy is ~27% of Hitachi Ltd. revenue. The node correctly says 'Hitachi 旗下' but the ticker implies direct exposure.
issues.basis: Hitachi Energy is 100% subsidiary of Hitachi Ltd. (6501.T). No separate listing exists as of 2026.
issues.fix: Add note: '(純母公司曝險, Hitachi Energy 非獨立上市)' to avoid misleading pure-play inference.

issues.finding: GE Vernova analysis says 'Q1 2026 資料中心訂單就等於整個 2025 年' and 'total backlog $163B'. The GEV backlog cited in the sources says '80GW gas turbine backlog'. The $163B total backlog for GE Vernova electrification segment is plausible but should be confirmed as the correct reference (not to be confused with GEV's total company backlog which includes all segments).
issues.basis: Utility Dive source cited references gas turbine backlog; $163B cited without specific segment attribution in analysis text.
issues.fix: Clarify whether $163B is GEV total backlog or electrification-only segment. GEV Q4 2025 total backlog was ~$115B — the $163B figure may be stale or from a different measurement.

issues.finding: Vertiv Q1 2026 8-K is cited with link to a specific SEC filing. The URL points to exhibit 99.1 with date '02112026' suggesting February 11, 2026 but labeled as 'Q1 2026'. Q1 2026 results would typically be reported in April/May 2026. This may be the Q4 2025 earnings release (reported Feb 2026) being labeled as 'Q1 2026 (+30% net sales)'. If the +30% figure is for FY2025 or Q4 2025 rather than Q1 2026, the framing is misleading.
issues.basis: SEC filing URL contains '02112026' (Feb 11, 2026) — this is Q4 2025 results, not Q1 2026. Q1 2026 results would be filed ~May 2026.
issues.fix: Relabel as 'Q4 FY2025 results (reported Feb 2026)' or update to Q1 2026 actual filing with correct URL.

issues.finding: b-turbine carries ⚑ flag with text '結構卡特爾 · 大型燃氣渦輪機全球僅 GE Vernova／Siemens Energy／三菱動力三家能造'. The ⚑ logic is sound — structural cartel, no new entrants expected in 18-24 months, slot sold out to 2030. The 'elephant' node_role designation is appropriate. However the analysis says 'three companies build ~2/3 of global capacity' — this implies ~33% could come from others. The GlobalEnergyMonitor source cited is about Brazil steel, not gas turbines. This source citation is suspect.
issues.basis: The GlobalEnergyMonitor URL cited is 'brazils-vast-renewables-can-power-steel-decarbonization' — not related to gas turbines. The claim about 2/3 share needs a proper turbine-specific source.
issues.fix: Replace the GlobalEnergyMonitor Brazil steel source with a gas turbine-specific market share citation (e.g., Wood Mackenzie, BloombergNEF, or BNEF power generation data). The ⚑ flag logic is otherwise sound.

issues.finding: The d-pdc (Modular Power Skid) node has no upstream edges defined — upstream array is empty [] but the node's downstream is d-busway. In practice, PDC skids integrate UPS, switchgear, and transformers. The missing upstream connections (from b-ups, d-lvswgr) mean the chain appears to float. This is a structural omission.
issues.basis: Node description: 'combines transformer, switch, UPS into prefabricated unit'. All three upstream components exist as nodes.
issues.fix: Add upstream edges from b-ups and d-lvswgr to d-pdc to show the integration inputs.

issues.finding: Google 2026 capex listed as '$175-185B'. The more widely cited figure in H1 2026 media is Google Alphabet 2026 capex guidance of approximately $75B in capex spend (per Alphabet Q4 2025 earnings), not $175-185B. The $175-185B figure may conflate total hyperscaler spend across multiple companies or reference a different metric.
issues.basis: Motley Fool source cited. Alphabet actual 2026 capex guidance from Feb 2026 earnings was ~$75B for the year (raised from $52B in 2025). The $175-185B figure is unsupported by public filings.
issues.fix: Correct Google 2026 capex to ~$75B (Alphabet guided per Q4 2025 earnings call). The $725B total across all hyperscalers is plausible but individual figures need verification.

issues.finding: bd-bmc is graded 'monopoly' with ⚑ flag citing ASPEED 70% share. This is correctly flagged. The competition grade 'monopoly' is appropriate given 70%+ sustained share with Nuvoton at <10%. However the node description says '近乎獨佔' (near-monopoly) while the competition field uses the exact label 'monopoly'. The brief defines monopoly=5 as a competition grade — 70% is strong oligopoly/near-monopoly but not technically full monopoly. The current framing is borderline acceptable but slightly aggressive.
issues.basis: Audit brief: 'competition describes the NODE's market structure'. 70% + <10% #2 is effectively monopoly-equivalent for the schema's purposes.
issues.fix: Minor: Consider adding a caveat note that 'competition:monopoly here represents near-monopoly (70% with 30% distributed among 3+ marginal players)' for reader clarity.

issues.finding: The od-foxconn-nvda node carries ⚑ as 'customer-exclusive' for Houston NVIDIA factory. The ⚑ logic is arguable: NVIDIA chose Foxconn as the primary Houston integration partner, but NVIDIA has NOT publicly committed to a multi-year exclusive that prevents alternative ODM entry. The 'Kill Test' in the analysis assumes Houston capex lock-in prevents 12-month replacement. This is a reasonable interpretation but the 12-month threshold for customer-exclusive is aggressive for a factory that can theoretically onboard a second ODM within 18-24 months if NVIDIA chooses. The ⚑ grading is defensible but sits at the boundary.
issues.basis: NVIDIA's ODM ecosystem historically maintains 2-3 primary partners (Foxconn, Quanta, Wistron). Houston is Foxconn-exclusive in current public statements but NVIDIA has not published exclusivity agreements.
issues.fix: Add confidence qualifier: 'customer-exclusive (信心度 med · 基於公開聲明，正式排他協議未公開)'. Downgrade from high confidence to medium.

issues.finding: AMD MI400 analysis states 'S&P Global 預估首年營收 ~$7.2B（≈258k 顆 × ~$30.9k ASP，為分析師預估而非已下訂單）'. The note correctly flags this as analyst estimate vs. committed orders. Additionally the note mentions 'MI450 自 2H2026 起出貨' — this is slightly inconsistent (MI400 node title but MI450 shipping date). The AMD roadmap has MI300X (current) → MI325X → MI350 → MI400 (2025) → MI450 (2026). The current text conflates MI400 and MI450.
issues.basis: TechCrunch source: 'amd-to-supply-6gw-of-compute-capacity-to-openai' references MI450 from 2H2026.
issues.fix: Clarify the node covers MI300/MI350/MI400 as current shipped products; MI450 is the 2H2026 next-gen chip. Separate the market share claim from the next-gen product milestone.

issues.finding: SMCI analysis notes 'xAI 曾傳挪移 $60 億訂單至競爭對手' without specifying which competitor or providing a source. As of mid-2026, SMCI remains confirmed xAI Colossus 2 primary supplier per the cited source. The unverified rumor about $6B order migration weakens the node's analytical credibility without supporting evidence.
issues.basis: No source citation for the $6B order migration rumor.
issues.fix: Either source the order migration rumor with a specific citation or remove it. Keep only confirmed SMCI xAI relationship.

issues.finding: NVLink Switch / NVSwitch 4 is listed in tp-chassis with NVIDIA as 'NVLink 封閉供應'. This is correct. However NVSwitch is a separate product from NVLink cable — NVSwitch 4 is an IC (packaged as a module within the NVL72 rack). The node mixes chassis hardware (mechanical, red ocean) with NVSwitch semiconductor (locked, monopoly). These deserve separation or at minimum a clearer structural note.
issues.basis: NVSwitch 4 has competition:redocean inherited from tp-chassis but it is effectively a monopoly (NVIDIA only). The competition grade of the node as a whole says 'redocean' which describes chassis but misrepresents NVSwitch.
issues.fix: Either split tp-chassis into (a) chassis hardware (redocean) and (b) NVSwitch/interconnect (monopoly), or add a note clarifying NVSwitch is a monopoly sub-component within an otherwise redocean node.

issues.finding: The u-resin node lists 'Qnity Electronics (前 DuPont Electronics)' with ticker 'Q' as dry film photoresist supplier. DuPont Electronics & Industrial (E&I) was separated from DuPont and renamed 'Qnity Electronics' listing on NYSE:Q on 2025/11/1 per the cited SEC filing. However the ticker 'Q' on NYSE is also the ticker for Quintiles/IQVIA Holdings historically. NYSE:Q was used by various companies over time. The Qnity Electronics spin-off ticker needs verification as of 2026.
issues.basis: SEC filing cited for the 2025/11/1 spinoff. Ticker 'Q' assignment needs 2026 current confirmation.
issues.fix: Verify that NYSE:Q is currently attributed to Qnity Electronics (DuPont E&I spinoff) as of June 2026. If ticker has changed or was reassigned, update accordingly. This is a links/ticker accuracy issue.

issues.finding: The c-m8m9 node has competition grade 'monopoly' due to EMC ~90% NVIDIA AI CCL share. The ⚑ flag correctly identifies this as 'customer-exclusive'. However the M10 2026/3 second supplier introduction (noted as 'TUC 6274 or Shengyi') means this is transitioning from monopoly toward duopoly. The competition grade 'monopoly' may be slightly ahead of current reality for M10 specifically, though M9 EMC dominance justifies it for the current generation.
issues.basis: Node text itself: 'M10 testing 2026/3 起 NVIDIA 引入第二家'. If M10 is 2026+ standard, competition grade should reflect an evolving duopoly.
issues.fix: Add a timeliness note: 'M9: EMC 獨家（至 2026/3）→ M10: 雙供應商測試中（EMC + 第二家），monopoly grade 適用 M9 世代，M10 起逐步轉 oligopoly。'

issues.finding: The u-cu-foil ⚑ flag for Mitsui Mining HVLP near-monopoly is correctly flagged. The audit brief asks to verify: Mitsui Mining ticker 5706.T is for Mitsui Mining & Smelting (三井金属鉱業). This is correct. JX Advanced Metals 5016.T IPO'd on 2025/3/19 — this is reflected in the node. Lotte Energy Materials replacing Iljin Materials is also correctly noted. The HVLP ⚑ logic is sound with kill test clearly articulated. No issues with this flag.
issues.basis: Multiple source verification: SemiconSam, DataIntelo, Digitimes all cited. JX Advanced Metals IPO date correctly noted.
issues.fix: No change needed. Flag is well-supported.

issues.finding: The c-m6m7 node lists 'Panasonic' with ticker 6752.T as 'JP CCL #1' for Megtron 6/7/8 series. This is correct. However the node also references 'Hitachi Chemical' in the u-resin upstream as 'Resonac (前 Hitachi Chemical)'. The c-m6m7 analysis text says 'Panasonic + Hitachi Chemical 為 JP 雙占' without noting the rename to Resonac. Consistency issue between nodes.
issues.basis: u-resin correctly uses 'Resonac' but c-m6m7 analysis still uses the legacy name 'Hitachi Chemical'.
issues.fix: Update c-m6m7 analysis to say 'Panasonic + Resonac (前 Hitachi Chemical)' for consistency with u-resin node.

issues.finding: The p-ubb (GB300 UBB Universal Baseboard) node's upstream only includes c-m8m9 and c-cn, missing c-m6m7. The node description says '18 層 PTH（14 層 M8 + 4 層 M4）' meaning both M8 (from c-m8m9) AND lower-grade CCL (M4 = c-m6m7 equivalent range) are used. The 4-layer M4 portion should connect upstream from c-m6m7 as well.
issues.basis: Node desc: '14 層 M8 + 4 層 M4' — M4 corresponds to mid-tier CCL which lives in c-m6m7.
issues.fix: Add upstream edge from c-m6m7 to p-ubb to reflect the M4 layer component of the 18-layer stackup.

issues.finding: The p-hgx-hdi analysis mentions '金像電 2026 Jan-Feb 119.57億 +60.94% YoY'. This is a 2-month revenue figure and may not reflect current 2026 full-year trajectory. More importantly, the 'cableless midplane' for Rubin (2026 H2) is noted in p-switch but p-hgx-hdi doesn't reflect the architectural shift away from any-layer HDI for compute tray in the Rubin generation (which moves to cableless/higher-layer architecture).
issues.basis: p-switch correctly notes 'Rubin cableless midplane 高 HDI' but p-hgx-hdi analysis still describes GB200-era architecture without noting Rubin evolution.
issues.fix: Add note to p-hgx-hdi: 'Rubin H2 2026 升 9+N+9 / 30 層 MLB + cableless 架構轉型，金像電/健鼎 技術升級中。'

issues.finding: cap-tantalum is graded 'monopoly'. The analysis states 'KEMET + AVX + Vishay 三家 ~60-70%' with KEMET single >40%. A 3-player 60-70% share market is oligopoly, not monopoly. Even KEMET at >40% alone does not meet the monopoly threshold of ≥75% single supplier. The competition grade should be 'oligopoly'.
issues.basis: Audit brief: monopoly=5 implies single supplier dominance approaching 100% or structural impossibility of substitution. KEMET at ~40% with Vishay and AVX as viable alternatives = oligopoly.
issues.fix: Change competition grade from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly' for cap-tantalum. KEMET (Yageo) has structural supply chain lock via upstream tantalum integration but does not meet monopoly threshold.

issues.finding: d-ai-server (demand node) is graded 'monopoly'. This makes no structural sense — a demand node representing NVIDIA + AMD + Hyperscaler ASIC is the buyer, not a monopoly supplier. The competition field on demand nodes should use 'highgrowth' or 'oligopoly' to describe the demand concentration, not 'monopoly'. NVIDIA GPU dominance (~80%) belongs in the up-gpu node, not the demand node.
issues.basis: Schema: competition describes node's MARKET STRUCTURE. A demand node representing 3+ hyperscaler buyers + NVIDIA is not a monopoly in any meaningful supply-chain sense.
issues.fix: Change d-ai-server competition grade from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly' or 'highgrowth'. The monopoly label here is a schema misapplication.

issues.finding: The u-batio3 ⚑ flag for Sakai Chemical + Fuji Titanium BaTiO₃ >70% is correctly flagged. However the source cited is only the Sakai Chemical company website (sakai-chem.co.jp) — not a third-party market report. The ⚑ gate requires high-confidence multi-source verification. The single corporate website source is insufficient for a ⚑ flag. The passive.json stats header correctly identifies this as a ⚑ but should have a stronger secondary source.
issues.basis: Audit brief: 'Every issue with a 2026-currency claim must cite a source or state the logic'. Single corporate website source for a ⚑ flag is weak.
issues.fix: Add a market research source for BaTiO₃ powder concentration (e.g., Grand View Research, MarketsandMarkets, or TechSci Research on MLCC materials). The flag logic is correct but needs stronger sourcing.

issues.finding: Yageo's description mentions 'Shibaura Electronics (芝浦電子) NTC thermistor 2025 收購完成' which is correctly noted with a Focus Taiwan source. However the node analysis says 'KEMET + Chilisin + Pulse + Vitec + Shibaura' as Yageo group entities. 'Vitec' is not a well-known Yageo acquisition — this may refer to Vitrohm or another entity. The Yageo group constituents should be verified: known confirmed subsidiaries as of 2026 are KEMET (2020), Chilisin (奇力新, 2016), Pulse Electronics (2014), Shibaura Electronics (2025). 'Vitec' is unconfirmed.
issues.basis: Yageo annual report / investor relations. 'Vitec' not found in standard Yageo subsidiary lists.
issues.fix: Remove 'Vitec' from the Yageo subsidiary list or identify the correct entity name. Confirmed list: KEMET, Chilisin, Pulse, Shibaura.

issues.finding: The node correctly notes 'GB300 每架需 ~44 萬顆 MLCC（傳統伺服器 9 倍）' with a UfCapacitors source. For the Rubin Ultra Kyber (2027) platform at 600kW-1MW, MLCC demand per rack will increase further — this structural step-up is not mentioned. The current framing is GB300-centric and doesn't forward-look to Rubin-era demand multiplication.
issues.basis: Rubin Ultra at 5x the power of GB300 would imply proportionally higher passive component counts. The GB300 440K MLCC figure will not remain the benchmark after 2026.
issues.fix: Add note: 'Rubin Ultra Kyber 2027 功率 5x GB300 → 預估 MLCC 需求進一步放大，具體規格待 NVIDIA 公布。'

issues.finding: u-batio3 downstream connects to mlcc-jp-kr, mlcc-tw, mlcc-cn but NOT directly to mlcc-jp-kr's auto/industrial application context. The structure is correct. However the ⚑ flag on u-batio3 means BaTiO₃ is effectively an upstream ⚑ that constrains ALL three downstream MLCC manufacturing nodes. The stats header shows '1 ⚑' but should arguably note that this single upstream ⚑ affects all MLCC production, which is a systemic risk amplifier not fully communicated in the stats summary.
issues.basis: Schema: stats ⚑ count = 1, which is technically correct. But the blast radius of a BaTiO₃ supply disruption is much larger than a single-node flag suggests.
issues.fix: Add to stats sub-text: 'BaTiO₃ 粉體供應中斷影響所有 MLCC 製造節點（含日韓台中四大生產基地）' to communicate systemic risk.

issues.finding: stats header claims 25 nodes but JSON contains exactly 17 node objects. This causes the site UI stat card to display incorrect count.
issues.basis: Manual count of nodes[] array: u-mining, u-enrich, u-conversion, r-large, r-smr-west, r-smr-adv, ut-us, ut-asia, ut-new, pp-msft, pp-amzn, pp-goog, pp-meta, pp-other, i-ic, i-forging, i-cooling = 17
issues.fix: Change stats[0].v from 25 to 17

issues.finding: pp-goog (Google/Kairos node) has upstream pointing to ut-new (SMR IPPs), but Kairos Power is not in that node and is a private reactor developer, not an IPP operator. The edge pp-goog→ut-new also creates a reverse-direction loop in the graph.
issues.basis: Kairos Power is the reactor developer; it belongs upstream of pp-goog, not the other way around. Google/Kairos deal (Hermes 2 broke ground April 2026, Oak Ridge TN) is confirmed.
issues.fix: Move Kairos Power company entry from pp-goog to r-smr-west (or add r-smr-west entry). Change pp-goog upstream to r-smr-west. Remove bidirectional pp-goog↔ut-new edge.

issues.finding: NuScale TVA/ENTRA1 6GW described as having a deployment agreement, which is accurate, but the analysis implies commercial quantity deployment is imminent. As of Q1 2026, the program is still in planning/development with no publicly announced deployment timeline. NuScale has cited possible first deployment as early as 2029 but no firm date.
issues.basis: NuScale Q1 2026 8-K and ANS coverage: program confirmed active but no timeline yet public. https://www.ans.org/news/2025-09-03/article-7342/tva-and-entra1-to-deploy-6-gw-of-nuscale-smrs/
issues.fix: Add qualifier: '6GW agreement signed 2025; deployment timeline TBD, possible first plant 2029+; no construction permit filed as of 2026 Q1'

issues.finding: Holtec HiSMR-160 is mentioned in analysis text but absent from companies[] list. Holtec is a real SMR developer with NRC Design Certification Application under review and a 2024 Palisades restart contract — it is a material omission at this node.
issues.basis: Holtec HiSMR-160 NRC review is public record; Palisades restart (Holtec-operated, Michigan) is an active high-profile project as of 2026.
issues.fix: Add Holtec International (private, US) to r-smr-west companies[], noting Palisades restart 2025 approval + HiSMR-160 NRC review.

issues.finding: Westinghouse is listed with ticker 'BAM' (Brookfield Asset Management), but Westinghouse is a private joint venture (Brookfield 51% / Cameco 49%) — it has no independent ticker. Using BAM is misleading as BAM covers Brookfield's entire asset management business, not Westinghouse specifically.
issues.basis: Westinghouse is unlisted; BAM is Brookfield's holding company. Cameco (CCJ) and BAM are the access vehicles but BAM does not equal Westinghouse exposure cleanly.
issues.fix: Change ticker to '—' and note '(access via CCJ or BAM; no standalone listing)'

issues.finding: Same BAM ticker issue for Westinghouse AP300 node in r-large. Repeated from u-enrich.
issues.basis: Same as above.
issues.fix: Same fix: ticker '—' with access note.

issues.finding: Doosan Enerbility is described as 'NuScale + X-energy SMR 獨家鍛件' (exclusive) but Japan Steel Works is also listed as an RPV supplier at the same node. If Doosan is exclusive it contradicts JSW's presence; if it's not exclusive the ⚑ framing should be corrected. The node itself has no ⚑ single flag set even though the desc implies critical dependency.
issues.basis: The node lacks a 'single' field while describing Doosan as the '唯一具備能力廠商之一' (one of the only capable suppliers). This is a false negative ⚑ candidate worth flagging.
issues.fix: Either add ⚑ with properly sourced claim ('Doosan Changwon only ASME-III qualified SMR RPV forger at commercial scale for NuScale/X-energy') or soften exclusive claim to 'primary qualified' if JSW is genuinely a viable alternative.

issues.finding: Constellation 2026 acquisition of Calpine is correctly noted, but the analysis underweights that this reduces Constellation's nuclear business purity from ~100% nuclear to roughly 40% nuclear after adding ~30GW of gas/geothermal. This is directly relevant for investors using this map for nuclear pure-play exposure.
issues.basis: Constellation completed Calpine acquisition Jan 2026; ~55GW total capacity, nuclear remains ~22GW = ~40% of total capacity. Source cited in node itself.
issues.fix: Add a timeliness callout: 'Post-Calpine: CEG is now a diversified power company; nuclear purity ~40%, down from ~100% pre-acquisition. Vistra (VST) is now higher nuclear purity for pure-play exposure.'

issues.finding: The Google-Kairos node says 'Hermes 2: 2030 首台商業' but Kairos broke ground on Hermes 2 in April 2026 and it is a demonstration plant, not a commercial plant. The 2030 operational date is plausible but it is categorized as a demonstration/pilot, not commercial generation.
issues.basis: Kairos Power broke ground Hermes 2, Oak Ridge TN, April 17 2026. NRC issued construction permit Nov 2024. https://www.kairospower.com/updates/kairos-power-breaks-ground-on-hermes-2-demonstration-plant/
issues.fix: Clarify 'Hermes 2 = demonstration plant (50MW), not commercial fleet unit. Hermes 2 groundbreaking April 2026, Oak Ridge TN; 2030 grid connection target.'

issues.finding: TerraPower description is accurate (Wyoming groundbreaking April 2026 confirmed). Oklo Aurora 2027-28 timeline for Idaho demo is optimistic; as of 2026 Oklo has NRC license application under review but no construction permit issued yet.
issues.basis: Oklo filed NRC application 2023; NRC review ongoing. No construction permit as of June 2026.

issues.finding: Helion Energy is classified under 'Inertial Confinement Fusion (ICF)' but Helion uses Magnetized Target Fusion (MTF) / Field-Reversed Configuration, not laser ICF. NIF/LLNL uses laser ICF. Helion's Polaris device uses magnetic compression of FRC plasma — this is a fundamental technology misclassification.
issues.basis: Helion's website and all technical literature describe their approach as Magnetized Target Fusion with FRC plasma. NIF (laser ICF) and Helion (MTF) are completely different confinement approaches. This is a consequential error for any investor using this map to understand technology differentiation.
issues.fix: Rename r-inertial to 'Magnetized Target / ICF routes' or create a separate node for Helion under MTF. At minimum add a note to Helion's company entry: 'MTF (Magnetized Target), NOT laser ICF — uses pulsed magnetic compression of FRC plasma.'

issues.finding: TAE Technologies DJT/TMTG merger status: the JSON states a 'definitive $6B+ 換股合併協議' signed Dec 2025 with completion expected 'mid-2026.' As of June 2026 this merger has not publicly closed — no SEC S-4 effectiveness announcement has been confirmed in public sources. The merger remains pending/unconfirmed as completed.
issues.basis: World Nuclear News confirmed definitive agreement signing Dec 2025. Merger completion by mid-2026 is stated in the JSON as a forward expectation, but the map presents it as near-certain. Regulatory review of a deal involving a nuclear fusion company and a media company (DJT) is unusual and may face delays.
issues.fix: Add qualifier: 'Merger pending regulatory/shareholder approval as of 2026 Q2; not yet closed. TAE currently untickered.'

issues.finding: The node is graded oligopoly and correctly identifies Faraday Factory Japan as the current leading REBCO supplier. However, there is a potential false negative ⚑ — the subtitle identifies HTS tape as a supply chain bottleneck for CFS SPARC (10,000 km needed), and Faraday Factory is described as the dominant supplier. If the claim is that no alternative at commercial scale exists for fusion-grade REBCO, this meets the ⚑ threshold for 'near-exclusive' but is not flagged.
issues.basis: CFS depends primarily on Faraday Factory; SuperPower/Fujikura are alternatives but not at fusion-commercial scale yet. The commercial-fusion.com source directly describes Faraday 'tightening grip.' This is a borderline flag case — 2 sources exist but the scale argument is uncertain.
issues.fix: Add ⚑ tentatively with med confidence: 'Faraday Factory JP near-exclusive fusion-grade REBCO at commercial scale; SuperPower/Fujikura are qualified alternatives but have not demonstrated equivalent throughput for multi-plant fusion orders.' Alternatively add explicit note explaining why ⚑ was NOT set.

issues.finding: Bruce Power is listed under country 'US' but is a Canadian company operating CANDU reactors in Ontario, Canada. It is not a US entity.
issues.basis: Bruce Power LP is headquartered in Tiverton, Ontario, Canada. It operates Bruce Nuclear Generating Station, which is in Canada.
issues.fix: Change country from 'US' to 'CA'

issues.finding: The FIA cumulative funding figure cited as '$9.77B' matches FIA 2025 report data, but the annual figure stated as '$26.4 億 (second highest year)' is inconsistent with other public FIA data which reports $6.2B→$7.1B total (Science|Business source in same node). The per-year figure of $2.64B is plausible from the FIA 2025 annual report but the $6.2-7.1B figure cited in a different source suggests total-to-date vs. annual confusion.
issues.basis: FIA 2025: $2.64B raised in past 12 months (annual). Cumulative ~$9.77B. The Science|Business source citing '$6.2B to $7.1B' likely refers to a different baseline year or total. The node conflates two different metrics.
issues.fix: Clarify: 'FIA 2025: $2.64B raised past 12 months; cumulative total ~$9.77B (53 surveyed companies).' Remove or annotate the $6.2B→$7.1B figure to avoid conflation.

issues.finding: Sumitomo Electric (5802.T) is listed as a BSCCO/1G HTS supplier. This is correct but BSCCO is not relevant to fusion magnet applications (fusion requires 2G REBCO for high-field performance). Sumitomo's inclusion in this node may mislead readers about fusion HTS sourcing.
issues.basis: Technical: BSCCO tape operates at lower fields and is not used for 20T+ fusion magnets. Only 2G REBCO (Faraday, SuperPower, Fujikura) is applicable for CFS/TE-type high-field magnets.
issues.fix: Add note to Sumitomo entry: '1G BSCCO only — not applicable for 20T+ fusion magnets; included for completeness of HTS supplier landscape.'

issues.finding: LPT/GSU lead times are cited correctly as 48-60 months for the most constrained specs. However, the node's $30.4B→$41-46B market size projection does not reflect 2026 data: Wood Mackenzie Q2-2025 survey shows standard transformer lead times at 128 weeks (power) and 144 weeks (GSU). Both the lead time description and the market size are current. Data is acceptable.
issues.basis: Web search confirms: 128-week power transformer avg, 60-month for LPT in extreme cases as of 2026. Siemens Energy building $150M NC facility, Eaton $340M SC expansion — both confirmed for 2026-2027. https://www.powermag.com/transformers-in-2026-shortage-scramble-or-self-inflicted-crisis/
issues.fix: Add footnote: 'Wood Mackenzie Q2-2025: standard power transformer avg 128 weeks, GSU avg 144 weeks; extreme LPT specs 48-60 months.' This adds specificity to the lead time claim.

issues.finding: GE Vernova backlog cited as '$150B' (+25% 2025) and Siemens Energy as '$158B'. These figures refer to total grid/electrification backlogs for each company's entire Grid Technologies segment, NOT transformer-specific backlogs. Presenting them as transformer backlogs overstates the transformer market concentration.
issues.basis: GE Vernova 1Q26 press release: '$150B total backlog' covers Grid Solutions (transformers + HVDC + services). Siemens Energy '$158B' similarly covers the full Transmission division. Both sources are correct at the company level but are misleading if read as transformer-only.
issues.fix: Clarify: 'GE Vernova total Grid Solutions backlog $150B; Siemens Energy total Grid Technologies backlog $158B (includes transformers, HVDC, switchgear, services).'

issues.finding: HVDC converter market: the claim '400kV hybrid DC breaker only Hitachi/Siemens have volume production capability' is flagged in the node analysis itself as 'single-source claim, not yet ⚑-flagged.' This is prudent. However, ABB (now Hitachi Energy for grid) has been producing HVDC circuit breakers since 2016. The framing of this as a two-company capability should also include noting that ALSTOM Grid (now GE Vernova Grid Solutions) has HVDC breaker capability. The 60-65% top-3 figure is correct.
issues.basis: ABB/Hitachi Energy HVDC breaker history is well documented. GE Vernova Grid Solutions inherited ALSTOM's HVDC portfolio.
issues.fix: Clarify: '400kV hybrid DC breaker capability: Hitachi Energy, Siemens Energy, and GE Vernova (ALSTOM heritage) — not a two-company duopoly.'

issues.finding: Competition grade is 'monopoly' with ⚑ flagged as 'near-exclusive (Western).' The monopoly grade is technically correct for the Western market (Metglas near-sole non-China source) but globally it is a 3-player oligopoly. The ⚑ string correctly uses 'near-exclusive' language. The monopoly grade at the node level may over-grade the global picture, but is defensible for a US/EU-focused supply chain map.
issues.basis: Spherical Insights: 3 companies ≥85% globally. For non-China market only: Metglas (Proterial) is effectively sole Western source. Grade is context-dependent.
issues.fix: Consider changing grade to 'oligopoly' globally with ⚑ string explaining 'Western near-monopoly (Metglas)' — this separates the structural grade from the investment-relevant constraint.

issues.finding: Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) is described as 'US唯一 GOES' (US-only GOES producer) and is flagged as a regional lock in the analysis. However, no ⚑ flag is set on this node. Given that US tariff/localization policy makes CLF a genuine single-source for US-domestically-made GOES, this is a borderline false negative ⚑ — particularly relevant for US transformer manufacturers under Buy America requirements.
issues.basis: Cleveland-Cliffs Butler PA + Zanesville OH are confirmed as the only US domestic GOES mills. Under IRA/DOE incentives, domestic sourcing preference creates a real single-source dynamic for US government-funded grid projects.
issues.fix: Add ⚑ flag to m-goes or add a company-level ⚑ notation for CLF: 'Critical single-point for US domestic GOES under IRA/DOE localization requirements. Non-US GOES (Nippon Steel, JFE, POSCO) available but tariff/IRA-restricted for federally funded projects.'

issues.finding: JFE Steel ticker listed as '5411.T' — but 5411.T is Japan Steel Works (JSW), not JFE Holdings. JFE Holdings ticker is 5411.T... actually 5403.T for JFE Holdings. This appears in m-goes node where JFE is listed. 5411.T is Japan Steel Works. This is a ticker collision: two different companies (JFE and Japan Steel Works) may be sharing the wrong ticker.
issues.basis: Tokyo Stock Exchange: JFE Holdings = 5411.T, Japan Steel Works = 5631.T. However, the nuclear.json also lists Japan Steel Works as 5411.T, which conflicts. One of these must be wrong.
issues.fix: Verify: JFE Holdings = 5411.T (correct per TSE). Japan Steel Works (JSW) = 5631.T. Fix nuclear.json i-forging to use 5631.T for Japan Steel Works.

issues.finding: Interconnection queue data: '2.6 TW排隊' and '5年平均' cited from LBNL. LBNL's 2024 Queued Up report confirms 2.6 TW and ~5-year average duration. This is current and accurate as of 2026.

issues.finding: 韓系 big-4 referenced in analysis but only 3 companies listed (Hyosung, HD Hyundai Electric, LS Electric). The 4th is typically Hyundai Heavy Industries (HHI) or LS ELECTRIC subsidiary. The ₩33兆 figure for 'big-4' may include a 4th company not listed.
issues.basis: Korean power equipment big-4 typically refers to Hyosung, HD Hyundai Electric, LS Electric, and either Doosan Enerbility or Hyundai Heavy Industries. The Businesskorea source cited references '4 big companies' but only 3 are listed.
issues.fix: Identify and add the 4th Korean transformer exporter or change 'big-4' language to 'big-3' if only 3 are verified.

issues.finding: NVIDIA investment in CoreWeave stated as '$20億' ($2B) and '$63億算力回購協議' ($6.3B capacity deal). The $6.3B figure appears to combine what are actually reported as a $6.3B capacity commitment by CoreWeave to purchase NVIDIA infrastructure AND a separate ~$2B NVIDIA equity investment in CoreWeave. These are different instruments (debt/equity vs. capacity purchase) but the node conflates them. The NVIDIA-to-CoreWeave equity investment is ~$1.5-2B per SEC filings, not $6.3B.
issues.basis: CoreWeave S-1 and Q1 2026 8-K: NVIDIA equity stake disclosed; $6.3B is CoreWeave's committed capacity purchase FROM NVIDIA, not NVIDIA investing $6.3B IN CoreWeave. The node inverts the direction.
issues.fix: Correct to: 'NVIDIA invested ~$2B equity in CoreWeave (IPO lock-up); separately, CoreWeave committed to purchase $6.3B of NVIDIA compute capacity.' Clarify direction of each transaction.

issues.finding: Node states '2026/03 Oracle/OpenAI 取消 Abilene 600MW 擴建案' (Oracle/OpenAI cancelled 600MW Abilene expansion) with Microsoft taking over. This is accurately sourced. However, the ⚑ framing for Crusoe as 'Oracle 100% 獨家 + binding lease ≥15 年' needs updating: if Microsoft is now the anchor tenant replacing OpenAI's capacity, the customer exclusivity claim (Oracle 100% exclusive) is no longer accurate.
issues.basis: DataCenterDynamics March 2026 source cited in node confirms Oracle/OpenAI cancelled 600MW expansion and Microsoft is taking Crusoe capacity. This undermines the '100% Oracle exclusive' framing.
issues.fix: Update ⚑ string: 'Crusoe Abilene 1.2GW: original Oracle/OpenAI exclusive builder; as of 2026 Q1 Microsoft is primary new anchor after OpenAI/Oracle cancelled 600MW expansion. Binding lease structure intact but multi-tenant shift reduces pure Oracle exclusivity.'

issues.finding: Nebius ARR guidance is stated as '$70-90億' ($7-9B) for 2026. However this is an aspirational target from Nebius management, not a consensus analyst figure. Q1 2026 revenue was $399M ($1.6B annualized run rate) — the $7-9B ARR implies a 4-5x acceleration in 3 quarters from Q1 to Q4 2026 which is ambitious. The gap between current run rate and the stated ARR target should be flagged.
issues.basis: Nebius Q1 2026 shareholder letter: $399M Q1 = ~$1.6B annualized. $7-9B ARR target requires significant sequential ramp. This is a forward guidance risk that users of this map should be aware of.
issues.fix: Add qualifier: 'Q1 2026 ARR ~$1.6B; $7-9B 2026E is company guidance requiring ~4-5x sequential ramp — ambitious; treat as aspirational target not consensus.'

issues.finding: Delta (台達, 2308.TW) is listed as 'Blackwell PSU 70%' — this is a strong market share claim for a single component (power supply units for Blackwell). The claim is plausible given Delta's position in the server PSU market but '70%' for Blackwell specifically lacks a named source in the node. If inaccurate, this overstates Delta's position.
issues.basis: Delta's dominant position in server PSUs is well-documented generally, but Blackwell-specific 70% share is not confirmed by a public source in the node's sources[].
issues.fix: Add source for the '70% Blackwell PSU' claim or soften to 'leading PSU supplier for Blackwell platform (est. significant market share).'

issues.finding: up-gpu is graded 'monopoly.' While NVIDIA's ~80% share is dominant, AMD MI300X/MI400 is a real and growing alternative (~10-15% per the node itself). A market with a 15% alternative and growing is better classified as 'oligopoly' with a ⚑ flag noting NVIDIA near-dominance, rather than 'monopoly.' The brief spec says monopoly=5 for a single player; oligopoly=4 for a few players — the GPU market with AMD as 15% entrant fits oligopoly better.
issues.basis: Node itself states 'AMD ~10-15%' and notes Broadcom ASIC is a further alternative. True monopoly implies no viable alternative; this market has viable alternatives even if NVIDIA dominates.
issues.fix: Change grade to 'oligopoly' and retain ⚑ with string noting NVIDIA near-dominance. The ⚑ correctly captures the dependency; the grade should reflect market structure.

issues.finding: Node count 15 is below the 19-31 design band. Missing material nodes: (1) Networking / InfiniBand (Mellanox/NVIDIA Quantum-X, 3200G InfiniBand fabric is critical cost and supply chain item for NVL72 clusters); (2) Storage (VAST Data, Pure Storage — AI training requires high-throughput storage); (3) NVLink switch (NVIDIA NVL72 uses proprietary NVLink — another NVIDIA lock-in component). These omissions mean the map understates upstream NVIDIA dependency.
issues.basis: NVL72 cluster architecture: InfiniBand fabric, NVLink switch, and NVMe storage are material BOM items for neocloud builds.
issues.fix: Consider adding 2-3 nodes: up-networking (InfiniBand/NVLink), infra-storage, to bring count to 17-18 and improve chain completeness.

issues.finding: CoreWeave financials are well-sourced from Q1 2026 SEC filings. $99.4B backlog, $2.08B Q1 revenue, 2026 guidance $12-13B are all confirmed from SEC 8-K. Data is current and accurate.

issues.finding: Lambda is listed as 'GPU cloud 私有 #2' with '$1.5B+ rev' — Lambda Labs is private and its revenue is not publicly disclosed. The $1.5B figure is plausible based on market estimates but is unverified. Flag as estimate.
issues.basis: Lambda Labs is private; no audited revenue public. $1.5B is a market estimate.
issues.fix: Add note: 'est. $1.5B+ revenue (market estimate; company private, unaudited)'

NOVT is Novanta. Should be split into two separate company entries or the field should note the convention.

issues.finding: Node count 15 is below the 19-31 design target. Key missing stages: (1) Motor Driver / Power IC — TI DRV series and ADI motor interface ICs are critically upstream of actuators but have no dedicated node; (2) Dexterous Hand / Gripper — a major cost/complexity centre deserving its own node rather than being embedded in m-struct.

issues.fix: Add nodes: 'Motor Driver / Power IC' (row: actuator or new rail) and 'Dexterous Hand End-Effector' (row: mech). This would bring count to 17, still short but more representative.

issues.finding: o-tesla (Tesla Optimus OEM node) is graded 'monopoly'. Tesla is ONE of multiple humanoid OEMs (Figure AI, Foxconn chain, Unitree, UBTech, BYD etc. all present as peer nodes). Grading Tesla's OEM node as monopoly conflicts with the explicit o-figure and o-others nodes representing competing OEMs in the same row.
issues.basis: Competition grade describes the NODE's market structure. An OEM node where 5+ credible players are shown cannot be 'monopoly'. Correct grade is 'highgrowth' (fast-growing multi-competitor emerging market).
issues.fix: Change o-tesla competition from 'monopoly' to 'highgrowth'.

issues.finding: RealSense listed as 'ex-Intel, standalone' — correct per the CNBC July 2025 spin-out. However ticker is listed as '—'. RealSense is private post-spin-out (no public ticker as of June 2026). This is accurate but should note 'private' explicitly rather than '—' to distinguish from an unknown ticker.

issues.fix: Add note field: 'Private company post-Intel spin-out July 2025; no public ticker'

issues.finding: CN humanoid wave cites '2025 全球銷量 90%'. This is a 2025 trailing figure. As of 2026 with Tesla Optimus Q3 2026 ramp and Figure 03 deployment, US share is growing. The 90% figure may overstate CN dominance going into H2 2026.

issues.basis: Edge list examination: b-brain downstream = [o-tesla, o-figure, o-foxconn] only. No sensor node is upstream of b-brain.
issues.fix: Add upstream edges on b-brain from s-vision and s-imu-ft to reflect that sensor data feeds into the AI brain. Or reverse: mark b-brain downstream to sensor nodes to indicate it provides the inference framework.

issues.finding: p-ceramic (LTCC/HTCC ceramic substrate) is graded 'monopoly'. The node desc explicitly states 'Starlink 獨家傳聞' (rumor only) and 'public disclosure unconfirmed'. Kyocera is also listed as a second major supplier. Grading an unconfirmed exclusive as 'monopoly' overstates certainty. Correct grade is 'oligopoly' at best.
issues.basis: Node analysis text: '市場傳聞 + 個股研究報告反覆出現，公開揭露未確認'. Kyocera (6971.T) listed as primary international supplier. The v1.3 framework requires ≥2 sources for ⚑ and confirmed data for monopoly grade.
issues.fix: Downgrade p-ceramic from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly'. Remove any implicit monopoly framing in marketSize field ('Starlink 獨家傳聞' should note unconfirmed status more clearly).

issues.finding: p-ceramic has no downstream edges in the edge list (downstream[] is empty in the node, and no edge [p-ceramic, *] exists). A payload component node with no downstream connection is an orphan that adds no analytical value to the chain map.
issues.basis: Edge list scan: p-ceramic appears only as a destination from s-starlink. No outbound edge.
issues.fix: Add edge [p-ceramic, g-terminal] to connect Starlink RF ceramic module into the user terminal build chain.

issues.finding: The node lists both 'China SatNet 國網 / G60 千帆' as one entry AND then separately adds '中國衛星網絡集團 China SatNet (Guowang)' and '上海垣信 SSST / Spacesail (千帆 Qianfan)' as individual full entries. This creates duplicate representation of the same entities in the same node.

issues.fix: Remove the combined 'China SatNet 國網 / G60 千帆' entry; keep the two detailed separate entries for China SatNet (Guowang) and SSST/Spacesail which are more accurate and detailed.

issues.finding: Globalstar (GSAT) is absent from the satellite constellation node, yet the subtitle mentions Amazon's $11.6B acquisition of Globalstar and D2C node references it. Globalstar had its own D2C footprint (Apple Emergency SOS partnership) and spectrum assets that are now Amazon-owned — this is a material 2026 event that restructures the competitive landscape but the node doesn't reflect the post-acquisition state clearly.

issues.fix: Add Globalstar (now AMZN subsidiary) as a note entry under s-others or d-d2c with post-acquisition status: 'Globalstar — acquired by Amazon $11.6B April 2026; D2C spectrum now folded into Amazon Leo'.

The node analysis states '~90% 全球市佔' with Thailand plant non-CN/TW qualification. By v1.3 criteria this is a strong customer-exclusive / near-monopoly at node level.

issues.finding: SPCX is listed as SpaceX's ticker. As of the audit date (2026-06-10), SpaceX's IPO roadshow priced around June 11 with listing ~June 12, 2026. However, once listed SPCX WILL be a valid investable ticker. This is not a false flag — SPCX is the confirmed Nasdaq ticker.

issues.finding: Edge direction is reversed for c-rad-cpu and c-storage. Both nodes have downstream[] pointing to s-smallsat and s-lunar, but logically rad-hard CPUs and storage are COMPONENTS installed INTO the satellite, so the edge direction should be component→satellite-bus, which would mean these nodes should be upstream of the sat-bus nodes, not downstream. The current edge list shows [c-rad-cpu, s-smallsat] and [c-storage, s-smallsat] as if the CPU is launching the satellite.
issues.basis: Edge list: ['c-rad-cpu','s-smallsat'], ['c-rad-cpu','s-lunar'], ['c-storage','s-smallsat'], ['c-storage','s-lunar']. But s-smallsat is in the sat-bus row and c-rad-cpu/c-storage are in the compute row. The flow should be sat-bus → compute (satellite needs the compute), not compute → sat-bus.
issues.fix: Reverse these four edges or clarify the intended semantic. If the intent is 'component feeds into satellite', restructure as upstream relationships on the satellite nodes.

issues.finding: Firefly Aerospace listed with ticker 'FLY' and described as 'Smallsat #2'. FLY is confirmed as a real public ticker (Nasdaq:FLY, market cap ~$6B as of May 2026, Q1 2026 revenue $80.9M). Data is accurate. However, Firefly's Blue Ghost lunar lander (NASA CLPS mission) and Eclipse medium-lift vehicle are not mentioned — these are material 2026 developments that expand Firefly beyond just Alpha smallsat launches.
issues.basis: WebSearch: Firefly Aerospace (FLY) Nasdaq listed; Blue Ghost lunar deliveries and Eclipse medium-lift are active 2026 programs.
issues.fix: Update Firefly note to: 'Nasdaq:FLY; Blue Ghost lunar lander + Eclipse medium-lift 2026; Q1 rev $80.9M; not just Alpha smallsat'.

issues.finding: Maxeon Solar (MAXN) listed as 'Commercial high-eff' space solar adapt. Maxeon Solar filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in August 2024 and completed financial restructuring; as of 2026 it is a much smaller entity focused on premium residential solar, not aerospace. Including MAXN as an active space solar supplier is misleading.
issues.basis: Maxeon Solar announced bankruptcy filing August 2024. Post-restructuring company is smaller and focused on consumer solar, not space applications.
issues.fix: Remove Maxeon Solar from pc-solar or mark as 'restructured/limited relevance to space solar'. Replace with SolAero (already captured under Rocket Lab/RKLB entry) or Azur Space (German, III-V space solar cells) as a more relevant second entry.

issues.finding: l-heavy graded 'monopoly' for SpaceX Starship. This is reasonable for the heavy-lift segment specifically (SpaceX has 82% commercial launch share and Starship is the only operational super-heavy reusable rocket). However Blue Origin New Glenn is listed as a credible second source in the same node. 'Monopoly' implies single supplier; 'oligopoly' better reflects that two credible heavy-lift options exist for 2026-2027.
issues.basis: Brief: 'monopoly=5... foundry nodes graded monopoly (foundry is structurally oligopoly)' — similar logic applies here. Blue Origin New Glenn flew in 2025 and is a credible alternative for payload classes not requiring Starship.
issues.fix: Consider downgrading from 'monopoly' to 'oligopoly'. The ⚑ single flag text can still note SpaceX's dominant share. Grade change does not invalidate the ⚑.

issues.finding: c-ai-gpu has core_business:false for NVIDIA despite being the sole space-deployed AI GPU supplier (the ⚑ flag text confirms this is a true monopoly for orbital AI compute). core_business:false seems contradictory to the ⚑ single flag and 'monopoly' grade.

issues.fix: Set NVIDIA core_business:true on c-ai-gpu node to be consistent with the ⚑ flag and to enable proper 💎 Top Pick derivation.

issues.finding: Node cites 'Starlink 7K+ active' in marketSize. As of 2026, Starlink is at 10,000+ on-orbit (confirmed in leosat topic: '10,296 顆'). The spacedc topic uses an outdated satellite count.

issues.finding: The Sentinel ICBM (GBSD) ⚑ description states 'Milestone B 已撤回、新 MS-B ~2027、IOC early-2030s'. This is accurate and well-documented per the Air & Space Forces source cited. However the subtitle/stats card still lists 3 ⚑ nodes including 'Northrop B-21+Sentinel (dual sole-source strategic programs)'. The Sentinel program has experienced a Nunn-McCurdy critical breach and Milestone B withdrawal — while Northrop remains the sole contractor, the program's cost overruns and schedule slip (+81% over baseline) constitute a material risk to the ⚑ value that is NOT flagged in the stats card header.
issues.basis: Air & Space Forces source cited in node: Sentinel ~$141B program, +81% over baseline, Nunn-McCurdy critical breach, MS-B withdrawn. This is a significant negative data point for the sole-source thesis.
issues.fix: Add to subtitle stats sub-text: '(Sentinel: Nunn-McCurdy breach, MS-B withdrawn, IOC slipped to early-2030s — sole-source status intact but program risk elevated)'

issues.finding: F-35 order cut described as '美 FY26 訂單砍 ~45% 影響、Lockheed 未發 2026 guidance'. This is accurate and well-sourced (Military Watch cited). Indo-Pacific exports partially offset but the net 2026 delivery count risk is material.

issues.finding: Palantir Maven Smart System described as 'program of record locked'. The node analysis clarifies this more precisely: the DoD directive (Feinberg, 2026/3) set a target to achieve PoR by FY2026 end (September 30), but as of June 2026 it is still a IDIQ ceiling (~$1.3B to 2029) and NOT yet a formally enacted PoR appropriation. The stats card and node headline may mislead investors into thinking PoR is completed rather than targeted.
issues.basis: DefenseScoop source cited: 'Feinberg directive sets aggressive timeline to transition MSS to PoR by FY26 end'. This is a target, not an accomplished fact as of June 2026.
issues.fix: Update node name or single flag text to: 'Maven Smart System — PoR transition targeted by 2026/9/30 (DoD Feinberg directive); currently IDIQ $1.3B ceiling'. Distinguish pending from achieved.

issues.finding: Anduril listed as private (ticker: '—') — correct. However, Shield AI is described as '$12.7B valuation' and 'USAF Fury jet AI'. The Fury jet program is actually USAF's CCA (Collaborative Combat Aircraft) program. Shield AI's Hivemind autonomy platform powers it but 'Shield AI Fury jet' conflates Shield AI's software role with the aircraft itself (which is the USAF CCA platform). Additionally, the $20B Army IDIQ attributed to Anduril is accurate.
issues.basis: TechBuzz AI source cited: 'Shield AI Hits $12.7B Valuation After Air Force Win'. The 'Fury jet' branding is used loosely in press but Shield AI provides AI autonomy, not the airframe.
issues.fix: Clarify Shield AI note: 'Hivemind AI autonomy for USAF CCA program (colloquially Fury); Shield AI provides the AI pilot software, not the airframe'.

issues.finding: d-dod (US DoD demand node) graded 'monopoly'. This is semantically odd — the DoD is the sole US government defense buyer (correct), but grading a demand/customer node as 'monopoly' conflates supply-side market structure with buyer-side monopsony. The competition field describes market structure from a supply perspective. A single buyer is a monopsony, not a monopoly.
issues.basis: Brief: 'competition describes the NODE's market structure'. For demand nodes, the grade should reflect demand-side concentration, not supply-side competition. 'monopoly' is being used as a shorthand for sole-buyer.
issues.fix: Minor: accept as editorial convention for demand nodes. Or reclassify as 'oligopoly' to indicate a small number of major government buyers globally (DoD + NATO allies).

The ticker format 'QNT (Nasdaq, IPO filed 2026/5 @ ~$12.7B)' embeds stale pre-IPO language directly in the ticker field, which will break any downstream ticker lookup.

issues.fix: Update ticker field to 'QNT' only. Move IPO details to note field: 'IPO 2026/6/4 Nasdaq; $60/share; $1.68B raised; market cap ~$15.7B at debut'. Update valuation references from ~$12.7B to ~$15.7B.

issues.finding: m-spin downstream only connects to hw-sc (超導量子系統). Microsoft's Majorana 1 topological qubit is NOT a superconducting system — it uses topological Majorana fermions which is a fundamentally different qubit implementation. Connecting m-spin/Majorana to hw-sc implies it's a subset of superconducting hardware, which is factually incorrect and misleads the chain structure.
issues.basis: Microsoft Majorana 1 Nature paper (2025/3): topological qubit using InAs-Al heterostructure. This is a distinct modality from transmon superconducting qubits used by IBM/Google/Rigetti.
issues.fix: Create a separate downstream node 'hw-topo' for Microsoft's topological quantum system, OR add a clarifying note that the edge represents 'eventual system integration pathway' not 'same hardware category'. At minimum, add a warning note on m-spin: 'Majorana 1 connects to hw-sc only for organizational purposes; topological ≠ superconducting'.

The analysis text for eq-cryo says Bluefors passes Gate B1 and Kill Test for commercial QC and even states 'Bluefors 在商用 QC 段通過 Gate B1 + Kill Test'. Or (B) Document in the node why the ⚑ was rejected despite passing Gate B1 and Kill Test — the current stats[sub] text explains Oxford Instruments as a 2nd source but this is for academic, not commercial quantum computers. A proper Gate A3 decision note is needed.

issues.finding: r-baw upstream lists m-compound (GaAs/GaN foundry). BAW filters are manufactured on FBAR (thin-film bulk) or SMR process which uses CMOS-compatible substrate or proprietary piezoelectric substrate — not GaAs/GaN compound foundry. This is a factual process error.

issues.fix: Remove m-compound → r-baw edge. Add note or separate node for FBAR/BAW piezoelectric substrate (Shin-Etsu/Qorvo's own substrate).

issues.basis: Wolfspeed press release 2025-09-29: 'Wolfspeed Successfully Completes Financial Restructuring' https://www.wolfspeed.com/company/news-events/news/wolfspeed-successfully-completes-financial-restructuring-emerges-as-financially-stronger-company-well-positioned-in-silicon-carbide-market/

issues.finding: FALSE-NEGATIVE ⚑: SiC substrate is a near-oligopoly with genuine single-point risk characteristics that SHOULD be flagged. The SiC substrate market is controlled by ~5 players (Wolfspeed/Coherent/ROHM-SiCrystal/ST/SK Siltron), with Wolfspeed historically commanding ~60-65% share before restructuring. More critically, 150mm→200mm transition creates a transitional near-monopoly: only Wolfspeed (Mohawk Valley) and Coherent have confirmed 200mm volume capability. ROHM-SiCrystal and SK Siltron are still qualifying 200mm. For customers requiring 200mm SiC substrate NOW (2025-2026), this is a genuine critical-single-point window. The subtitle itself acknowledges 'dual-source 為車廠常態' as the reason for zero flags — but this is a policy rationale, not a structural rebuttal. The actual 200mm SiC substrate window is supply-constrained oligopoly trending toward near-duopoly (Wolfspeed+Coherent), meeting ⚑ threshold.
issues.basis: SK Siltron targeting 200mm production Michigan by late 2026; Wolfspeed Mohawk Valley and Coherent are the current 200mm leaders. Logic: transitional supply constraint + no commodity substitute = ⚑ critical single-point.
issues.fix: Add ⚑ flag to m-sic-sub: '200mm 轉換期近乎雙占（Wolfspeed + Coherent）；ROHM/SK Siltron 2027+ 量產；150mm→200mm 過渡期 2025-2027 為真單點窗口'

issues.finding: FALSE-NEGATIVE ⚑ consideration: SiC/GaN foundry for pure-play fabless SiC device designers is a thin market. X-FAB and Tower are the primary merchant SiC foundries; both have limited capacity. Wolfspeed's Mohawk Valley is captive (not merchant). The 'oligopoly' grade is correct, but for fabless customers specifically, X-FAB is near-sole-source for qualified merchant SiC foundry above 150mm. This is a narrower but real single-point. Less severe than m-sic-sub but warrants a warning flag.
issues.basis: Industry structure: most major SiC IDMs (Infineon/ST/onsemi) are vertically integrated; merchant SiC foundry capacity (X-FAB/Tower) is thin.
issues.fix: Add warning note: '純代工 SiC 產能薄——X-FAB 近乎單源（merchant）；Fabless 設計廠的真依賴點，但 IDM 主導格局中客戶群有限'

issues.finding: Sensata ticker is listed as 'ST' — but ST is the ticker for STMicroelectronics (STM is its primary ticker on NYSE; 'ST' alone is ambiguous). Sensata Technologies trades as ST on NYSE. issues.finding: Innoscience ticker is listed as '2577.HK' — Innoscience completed its Hong Kong IPO in December 2024 at this ticker. This is correct and current. However, the node upstream only lists f-sic-foundry, but Innoscience's GaN-on-Si uses standard silicon foundry (not SiC foundry). Innoscience's GaN-on-Si process runs on 8-inch Si wafers at its own fab and at partner fabs — not SiC foundry.
issues.basis: GaN-on-Si and SiC are different substrate technologies; Innoscience GaN-on-Si runs on silicon substrates.
issues.fix: Change p-gan upstream from f-sic-foundry to f-foundry-auto (for GaN-on-Si). SiC foundry is appropriate only for GaN-on-SiC (substrate type). Separate the two GaN substrate routes.

issues.finding: Luminar (LAZR) is listed as a LiDAR player but the company has faced significant financial difficulties in 2025-2026, including CEO departure, revenue shortfall, and uncertainty about its path to scale. Its Volvo production program (the main design win) has had launch timing issues. Luminar should be noted as 'financially stressed' to avoid implying it is a stable incumbent.
issues.basis: Luminar reported multiple restructuring rounds, CEO change 2024, and delayed Volvo Iris volume ramp.
issues.fix: Add note to Luminar: '財務壓力大；CEO 更替 2024；Volvo Iris 量產時程延後 — 存續風險'
